answer: The Court ruled that the law violated the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights because it treated every citizen as a potential suspect, without any grounds for suspicion. Even though the goal was to fight terrorism, the Court found that blanket retention of everyone's communications data for up to two years was disproportionate and interfered with the right to private life and data protection more than necessary. It set the principle that security measures must be targeted and ruled out mass surveillance.
title: Data Retention Directive

question: Did the annulment make it harder to investigate crime in Europe?
answer: Law enforcement agencies across the EU have said yes. Europol has reported that the loss of retained data has hindered investigations into organised crime, child sexual abuse, and terrorism. The UK's National Crime Agency warned in 2015 that 87% of serious crime cases used communications metadata. But privacy advocates argue that targeted preservation orders, issued per case with a warrant, can replace blanket retention without the privacy harms.
title: Data Retention Directive

question: What difference did this ruling make for later EU privacy laws, like the GDPR?
answer: The CJEU's reasoning in Digital Rights Ireland directly shaped how the EU protects data. The ruling established that blanket data retention is a "particularly serious interference" with fundamental rights. This principle was later cited in the Court's 2015 Schrems ruling that invalidated the Safe Harbour agreement for US data transfers, and in the 2020 Schrems II ruling that invalidated Privacy Shield. The GDPR, which came into force in 2018, strengthened the role of data protection authorities and created stronger enforcement mechanisms, in part as a reaction to the failures shown by the Data Retention Directive's annulment.
title: Data Retention Directive

question: Why did EU rules focus on hedge funds and private equity instead of regular mutual funds?
answer: The UCITS Directive (2009/65/EC) had already created a harmonized regulatory framework for retail-oriented mutual funds, allowing them to market across the EU with a single authorization. Alternative funds — hedge funds, private equity, real estate — were entirely outside this regime, regulated at the national level or not regulated at all. The 2008 crash showed these unregulated pockets could generate systemic risk through leverage and opaque derivatives exposures that UCITS funds could not hold.
title: Directive 2011/61/EU on Alternative Investment Fund Managers

question: Why did the UK strongly oppose EU rules for hedge funds and private equity managers?
answer: The UK hosted about 80% of EU-based alternative fund managers, primarily in London. The UK government and Financial Services Authority argued the directive would drive these funds to non-EU jurisdictions like the Cayman Islands or Switzerland, costing London its competitive advantage. British MEPs led amendments to limit the directive's extraterritorial reach and protect the London-based prime brokerage industry. The UK's eventual support came only after gaining opt-outs on key points, including national private placement regimes during the transition period.
title: Directive 2011/61/EU on Alternative Investment Fund Managers

question: Why did EU rules for private equity firms include limits on selling off a company's assets?
answer: The European Parliament pushed for these provisions following political pressure from German and French socialist MEPs who cited cases where private equity firms acquired manufacturing companies, loaded them with debt, and paid themselves dividends from borrowed funds before selling the stripped-down company. The Fairchild Dornier bankruptcy (2002) and the 2007 collapse of German retailer Hertie were frequently referenced during parliamentary debates. The two-year ban on capital reductions was framed as protecting creditors and employees.
title: Directive 2011/61/EU on Alternative Investment Fund Managers

question: How did EU rules for hedge funds and private equity affect fund managers based outside the EU?
answer: The directive created a "third-country passport" system that non-EU managers could apply for from ESMA, but only if their home jurisdiction had equivalent regulatory standards. The first passport applications were accepted only in 2016, and by 2018, fewer than 10 non-EU managers had obtained passports. Most non-EU managers relied on national private placement regimes (NPPRs) available in key markets like the UK, Germany, and France, or used reverse solicitation. The delay in passport availability was a major frustration for US and Asian managers.
title: Directive 2011/61/EU on Alternative Investment Fund Managers

question: Why do large financial groups need special oversight beyond normal banking and insurance rules?
answer: Because risks can hide in the cracks between sectors. A bank owned by an insurance company might make loans that the insurance parent quietly guarantees, shifting risk off the bank's balance sheet. Capital that looks adequate for the bank alone and adequate for the insurer alone might be the same capital counted twice — "double gearing." The 2008 crisis showed that no single national authority was looking at the whole picture. Fortis had both a bank and an insurer under the same roof, but the bank regulator didn't know what the insurer was doing, and vice versa. When the bank ran into trouble, the insurer's capital was already committed, and the whole structure collapsed.
title: Directive 2011/89/EU amending Directives 98/78/EC, 2002/87/EC, 2006/48/EC and 2009/138/EC as regards the supplementary supervision of financial entities in a financial conglomerate

question: What actually happened with Fortis that triggered this rule change?
answer: Fortis was a Belgian-Dutch financial group with both a major bank and a major insurer. During the 2008 crisis, Fortis had used its insurance arm to provide credit guarantees to its banking arm, effectively shifting risk internally without reporting it to either the banking or insurance supervisor. When credit markets froze, those guarantees were called, and the insurance arm couldn't cover them because its capital was already supporting the bank. No single regulator had the legal authority to demand consolidated data on intra-group exposures. Fortis needed an EUR 11.2 billion state rescue split between three countries — then collapsed anyway and was nationalised and broken up. That demonstrated that voluntary, sector-by-sector supervision was dangerously inadequate.
title: Directive 2011/89/EU amending Directives 98/78/EC, 2002/87/EC, 2006/48/EC and 2009/138/EC as regards the supplementary supervision of financial entities in a financial conglomerate

question: Does every European country have to apply these rules the same way?
answer: In principle yes, but in practice enforcement varies. The directive sets minimum standards, and national authorities can apply stricter rules. The UK, before Brexit, was the most aggressive enforcer, requiring conglomerates to hold additional capital buffers beyond the directive's minimums. Germany and Italy were slower to implement, and the European Commission had to push them with infringement procedures. Smaller countries like Luxembourg, which hosts many financial holding companies, were initially reluctant to identify all conglomerates operating from their territory because of competitive pressures — until the European Banking Authority and EIOPA began publishing common lists of identified groups.
title: Directive 2011/89/EU amending Directives 98/78/EC, 2002/87/EC, 2006/48/EC and 2009/138/EC as regards the supplementary supervision of financial entities in a financial conglomerate

question: Can a crime victim get information about their case in another EU country?
answer: Yes. Under the directive, every victim has the right to receive information about their case in a language they understand from their first contact with authorities, including about available support services, how to make a complaint, and the progress of the investigation. This applies regardless of where in the EU the crime occurred.
title: Directive 2012/29/EU establishing minimum standards on the rights, support and protection of victims of crime

question: Does EU law require police to protect a domestic violence victim from the perpetrator?
answer: Yes. The directive requires police to conduct an individual risk assessment for every victim who may be at risk of repeat victimisation, intimidation, or retaliation. For domestic violence victims, this assessment must specifically address the risk from the intimate partner. Based on the assessment, authorities must provide protective measures such as restraining orders, separate waiting rooms in courts, or special interview arrangements.
title: Directive 2012/29/EU establishing minimum standards on the rights, support and protection of victims of crime

question: Can a victim appeal if a prosecutor decides not to press charges?
answer: Yes. Article 11 of the directive gives victims the right to have a decision not to prosecute reviewed by a competent authority. This does not mean victims can force a prosecution, but they must have access to a mechanism (administrative, judicial, or quasi-judicial) to challenge the decision. Several member states — including France and Germany — struggled with this provision because it conflicts with their traditional prosecutorial discretion.
title: Directive 2012/29/EU establishing minimum standards on the rights, support and protection of victims of crime

question: Why did Europe need a common cybersecurity law before 2016?
answer: Before the NIS Directive, each EU country set its own cybersecurity rules—if it had any at all. The 2014 Commission impact assessment found that only 7 member states had a national cyber incident response team. A cyberattack on a power grid in one country could spread to neighbours with no coordinated reporting, and no legal obligation to warn others. The 2015 Ukraine blackout—attributed to a state-sponsored Russian hacker group—was the direct trigger, showing that critical infrastructure was a soft target without common minimum protections.
title: Directive 2016/1148/EU on security of network and information systems (NIS Directive)

question: Which companies and sectors were most affected by the NIS Directive?
answer: The directive targeted "operators of essential services" (OES) in energy, transport, banking, financial market infrastructure, health, drinking water, and digital infrastructure. This meant companies like EDF (energy), Deutsche Bahn (transport), BNP Paribas (banking), and national water utilities had to implement "appropriate" security measures and report serious incidents. Digital service providers (cloud platforms, online marketplaces, search engines like Amazon Web Services, eBay, Google Search) had lighter obligations, a concession won by industry lobbyists.
title: Directive 2016/1148/EU on security of network and information systems (NIS Directive)

question: Did the NIS Directive actually improve cybersecurity?
answer: Partially, but inconsistently. The Commission’s 2020 review found that only 10 out of 27 member states had fully identified all their operators of essential services; others had partial lists. Only 32% of OES had fully implemented the required security measures by 2019. The directive significantly improved governance—every member state now has a national CSIRT and a competent authority—but enforcement was weak, with no major fines issued under NIS 1 itself. The UK (then still a member) issued no NIS-specific cybersecurity fines before the directive was replaced.
title: Directive 2016/1148/EU on security of network and information systems (NIS Directive)

question: Why was the NIS Directive replaced so quickly (by 2022)?
answer: The directive was viewed as too flexible. Member states could define "operators of essential services" differently, creating a patchwork of enforcement: Germany identified over 2,000 OES; Malta identified fewer than 20. The lighter rules for digital service providers created a "safe harbour" for big tech platforms. The 2020 impact assessment for the revision (NIS 2) noted that only 32% of OES had fully implemented security measures. A major ransomware attack on Ireland’s Health Service Executive in May 2021—which disrupted all national health IT systems for weeks—sealed the case for stronger, more harmonised rules.
title: Directive 2016/1148/EU on security of network and information systems (NIS Directive)

question: Why did Europe’s first cybersecurity law take three years to pass?
answer: Because national governments fiercely resisted giving the EU oversight of their critical infrastructure, especially in defence and financial sectors. The UK, Germany, and France each wanted to preserve their own cybersecurity agencies’ primacy. The three-year deadlock was only broken when the GDPR’s passage created a precedent for tough EU digital regulation, and when a series of high-profile attacks (Belgacom 2013, Bundestag 2015, Ukrainian power grid 2015) made inaction politically untenable.
title: Directive (EU) 2016/1148 concerning measures for a high common level of security of network and information systems across the Union (NIS Directive)

question: Does every EU country have to follow the same cybersecurity rules?
answer: Under the NIS Directive, no — and that was the problem. Member States had wide discretion to define "essential services," set their own identification thresholds, and choose their own enforcement approach. This created a patchwork: an energy company in Germany faced stricter oversight than one in Romania, even though both fed the same European grid. The NIS 2 Directive (2022/2555) fixed this by harmonising sector lists, incident reporting thresholds, and fine levels across all Member States.
title: Directive (EU) 2016/1148 concerning measures for a high common level of security of network and information systems across the Union (NIS Directive)

question: What happens if a company does not report a cyberattack?
answer: Under the NIS Directive, very little. The Directive required operators to report incidents that "significantly" disrupted services, but each Member State defined "significant" differently. No public fines were ever issued. The system was widely criticised as toothless. The NIS 2 Directive changed this drastically, making failure to report a punishable offence with fines up to €10 million or 2% of global turnover, and requiring initial reports within 24 hours.
title: Directive (EU) 2016/1148 concerning measures for a high common level of security of network and information systems across the Union (NIS Directive)

question: Does Europe have a law requiring public websites to be accessible for people with disabilities?
answer: Yes. The Web Accessibility Directive (2016/2102/EU) legally requires all public sector websites and mobile apps — from local council tax systems to national health service portals — to meet the European standard EN 301 549, which is based on the international WCAG 2.1 Level AA guidelines. This means features like text-to-speech compatibility, keyboard-only navigation, and colour contrast adjustments are mandatory by law, not optional best practice.
title: Directive (EU) 2016/2102 on the accessibility of the websites and mobile applications of public sector bodies

question: Why did it take so long for EU countries to agree on digital accessibility rules?
answer: National laws had been diverging since the 1990s — the UK led with the Disability Discrimination Act in 1995, while Germany and Italy passed their own laws in 2002 and 2004 — creating a patchwork of inconsistent standards. The EU could not harmonise accessibility purely as a social policy because member states blocked it. The game-changer was the digital single market strategy, which reframed accessibility as an internal market problem: divergent laws cost businesses an estimated €145–255 million annually. Once the Commission tied it to market efficiency, the political calculus shifted.
title: Directive (EU) 2016/2102 on the accessibility of the websites and mobile applications of public sector bodies

question: Are companies or private websites covered by the EU's web accessibility law?
answer: No, but partly. The Web Accessibility Directive only applies to public sector bodies. However, the European Accessibility Act (2019/882/EU), which came into full effect in June 2025, extends similar obligations to many private-sector digital services — including e-commerce websites, banking apps, e-books, and online transport booking services. So while a private blog or a local bakery's website is not covered, large private digital platforms are now bound by comparable rules.
title: Directive (EU) 2016/2102 on the accessibility of the websites and mobile applications of public sector bodies

question: What happens if a public sector website in the EU is not accessible?
answer: Citizens can file a complaint with the national enforcement body designated by each member state — for example, the Federal Monitoring Board for Digital Accessibility in Germany or the Equality and Human Rights Commission in the UK (before Brexit). The directive requires every website to include an "accessibility statement" with a feedback mechanism. As of 2025, no EU country has issued a monetary fine for non-compliance, but several have sent formal warnings, and the Commission can launch infringement proceedings against member states for systematic failures.
title: Directive (EU) 2016/2102 on the accessibility of the websites and mobile applications of public sector bodies

question: Do EU government websites and apps have to be usable by people with disabilities?
answer: Yes, since 2018–2021, depending on the type of content. EU law requires all public sector websites and mobile apps — from national tax portals to local library catalogues — to meet technical accessibility standards (essentially WCAG 2.1 Level AA). This means they must work with screen readers, offer text alternatives for images, have sufficient colour contrast, and be navigable by keyboard alone. Each website must also publish an accessibility statement and let users report problems.
title: Directive (EU) 2016/2102 on the accessibility of the websites and mobile applications of public sector bodies

question: What happens if a government website fails accessibility checks?
answer: It depends on the member state. Each country must designate an enforcement body — such as France’s Interministerial Digital Directorate (DINUM) — that can investigate complaints and demand fixes. The Commission’s 2021 report found that fewer than half of member states had fully operational monitoring and complaint mechanisms. In practice, disabled users often rely on national ombudsman complaints or, in a few cases, lawsuits. A German court in 2022 ordered a city to fix its inaccessible parking permit system after a complaint.
title: Directive (EU) 2016/2102 on the accessibility of the websites and mobile applications of public sector bodies

question: Are private companies covered by the same rules as government websites?
answer: Not directly. The Web Accessibility Directive only covers public sector bodies. However, a separate law — the European Accessibility Act (2019/882/EU) — applies similar rules to private companies in key sectors like banking, e-commerce, transport, telecoms, and e-readers, from June 2025. Many private firms that win government contracts also have to comply as a procurement condition.
title: Directive (EU) 2016/2102 on the accessibility of the websites and mobile applications of public sector bodies

question: Which European countries are best and worst at enforcing these rules?
answer: According to the Commission’s 2021 implementation report, the Netherlands, Sweden, and France have the most robust monitoring systems and regularly publish compliance data. At the other end, Bulgaria, Greece, and Romania had not yet designated enforcement bodies or conducted any systematic checks. The Commission has not launched formal infringement cases against any member state for non-compliance as of 2023, despite documented gaps.
title: Directive (EU) 2016/2102 on the accessibility of the websites and mobile applications of public sector bodies

question: Why did the EU need a third update to its rules on sharing government data?
answer: Because the 2013 directive allowed member states to charge above marginal cost for most data, creating a fragmented market where the same meteorological data cost €500 in one country and €50,000 in another. The Commission's 2018 impact assessment found that this patchwork suppressed the data economy by roughly 60% of its potential. The new directive closed that loophole for high-value datasets and harmonised charging to marginal cost for the rest, making cross-border re-use commercially viable.
title: Directive (EU) 2019/1024 on open data and the re-use of public sector information

question: Why are museums and libraries allowed to charge for reusing their digital collections?
answer: Libraries, museums, and archives have digitisation and preservation costs the Commission considered disproportionate. The directive allows these institutions to charge cost-plus (actual costs plus a reasonable return on investment) for commercial re-use of cultural data, while non-commercial re-use must be free. This compromise came after intense lobbying from the European Museum Association and the European Bureau of Library, Information and Documentation Associations, which argued in their 2017 joint submission that mandatory free re-use would bankrupt small museums.
title: Directive (EU) 2019/1024 on open data and the re-use of public sector information

question: Why were state-owned companies like railways and utilities suddenly required to share their data?
answer: The 2013 directive only covered public sector bodies. The Commission found that transport and utility companies (publicly owned or controlled) generated massive, commercially valuable datasets—train timetables, energy grid loads, port traffic—that were closed to re-use. Including them under the directive was championed by the European Parliament's rapporteur Neena Gill, who argued in a 2018 parliamentary working document that "publicly funded infrastructure data must be a common resource." The inclusion faced strong opposition from Deutsche Bahn and SNCF, but the Commission maintained that Article 114 TFEU gave it the power to regulate public undertakings in the internal market.
title: Directive (EU) 2019/1024 on open data and the re-use of public sector information

question: Why did the EU finally act after years of ignoring whistleblower protection?
answer: The combination of LuxLeaks, Panama Papers, and the murders of Caruana Galizia and Kuciak made the political cost of inaction unbearable. Juncker, personally implicated in the Luxembourg tax-ruling system, needed to demonstrate his Commission could deliver on tax fairness. The Parliament's Rozière resolution and a coordinated civil-society push gave Timmermans and Jourová the political room to propose a horizontal directive rather than the sector-by-sector patchwork DG FISMA had previously favoured.
title: Directive (EU) 2019/1937 on the protection of persons who report breaches of Union law

question: Why does EU whistleblower law let people report wrongdoing directly to the media?
answer: Civil society groups, citing the Caruana Galizia and Kuciak cases, argued that requiring internal reporting first would be lethal where employers or governments themselves were the wrongdoers. The compromise: whistleblowers can go public if internal or authority channels fail to act within set deadlines, if there is imminent danger to the public interest, or if reporting to authorities risks retaliation or evidence destruction. The European Court of Human Rights' Guja v. Moldova jurisprudence underpinned the legal logic.
title: Directive (EU) 2019/1937 on the protection of persons who report breaches of Union law

question: Why did Germany have so much trouble putting EU whistleblower rules into national law?
answer: Germany's draft law became hostage to a coalition fight over whether anonymous reports must be accepted and whether protection should extend to violations of national law beyond the EU minimum. Business associations including the BDA opposed scope expansion; the SPD-Greens-FDP coalition wanted it. The Bundesrat, controlled by CDU-led states, blocked the first version in February 2023. A reduced compromise text passed in May 2023, more than 17 months late, prompting a €5.4 million daily-penalty referral by the Commission that was eventually dropped.
title: Directive (EU) 2019/1937 on the protection of persons who report breaches of Union law

question: When I see a discount online, how do I know it's real?
answer: EU law now requires that any advertised price reduction must be based on the lowest price the seller charged in the previous 30 days. If a product was €50 for the last month and drops to €40, the discount is genuine. Sellers can no longer inflate the price for a week to claim a bigger "sale" price.
title: Directive (EU) 2019/2161 — Omnibus Directive: better enforcement and modernisation of EU consumer protection rules

question: Do sellers on Amazon or Etsy have to tell me if they're a business or a private seller?
answer: Yes. Online marketplaces must clearly display whether the seller is a "trader" (a business) or a private individual. This matters because EU consumer law gives you far stronger protections — like a 14-day right of withdrawal — when buying from a business. Private sellers are generally exempt from these rules.
title: Directive (EU) 2019/2161 — Omnibus Directive: better enforcement and modernisation of EU consumer protection rules

question: Can I get compensation if I'm misled by a fake review?
answer: Under the Omnibus Directive, you have a right to individual remedies — including a price reduction or contract termination — if you suffer harm from unfair commercial practices like fake reviews. Previously, only public authorities could act. This means you can seek compensation directly from the seller or platform, not wait for a regulator to fine them.
title: Directive (EU) 2019/2161 — Omnibus Directive: better enforcement and modernisation of EU consumer protection rules

question: What happens if a company breaks these rules across multiple EU countries?
answer: National consumer authorities now coordinate through the CPC network, and fines for EU-wide violations can reach 4% of the company's annual turnover in the member state where the violation was committed. This high maximum penalty is designed to deter large platforms from profiting from non-compliance.
title: Directive (EU) 2019/2161 — Omnibus Directive: better enforcement and modernisation of EU consumer protection rules

question: Why don't EU accessibility rules cover every product and service?
answer: The directive was a political compromise. The Commission initially considered a broader scope but faced fierce opposition from industry groups arguing that coverage of all consumer goods would be unworkable. The final list was restricted to products and services where the Commission's impact assessment showed the highest accessibility barriers and the strongest business case for harmonisation. Excluded sectors like housing, education, and public transport infrastructure fell under different EU or national competence.
title: Directive (EU) 2019/882 on the accessibility requirements for products and services

question: Why did big tech companies support EU rules on making products and services accessible?
answer: For major technology firms like Apple, Microsoft, and Google, the EAA offered a simple economic advantage: one set of rules for 27 countries instead of potentially 27 different national laws. The Commission's 2015 impact assessment found that a single EU standard could save companies up to €50 billion annually in compliance costs. These companies also had a strategic interest: accessible design expanded their market to include an estimated 80 million disabled Europeans. Apple, for example, publicly supported the directive, arguing that accessibility was a core design principle for its products.
title: Directive (EU) 2019/882 on the accessibility requirements for products and services

question: Why don't EU accessibility rules spell out exact technical standards?
answer: The EAA deliberately uses "functional accessibility requirements" — such as "the product must be perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust" — rather than mandating specific technical standards like "the button must be at least 20mm wide." This approach, championed by the European Committee for Standardisation (CEN) and the Commission, was designed to avoid obsolescence as technology evolves. The Commission's 2017 impact assessment explicitly noted that "technology-neutral language is essential to ensure the directive remains relevant for future innovations."
title: Directive (EU) 2019/882 on the accessibility requirements for products and services

question: Which products must be accessible under EU law?
answer: The European Accessibility Act applies to smartphones, tablets, computers, e-readers, payment terminals, ticketing machines, banking services, e-commerce websites, e-books, and audiovisual media services. It does not cover all consumer products — for example, household appliances like washing machines are excluded unless they have digital interfaces. Micro-enterprises with fewer than 10 employees and turnover under €2 million are exempt.
title: Directive (EU) 2019/882 on the accessibility requirements for products and services

question: Does EU law force every company to comply by 2025?
answer: Yes, but with phased deadlines. Products placed on the market after 28 June 2025 must meet the requirements. However, companies can still sell existing stock manufactured before that date. Member states could also apply transitional periods for certain products — for instance, Greece and Cyprus secured a two-year extension for self-service terminals due to their small market size.
title: Directive (EU) 2019/882 on the accessibility requirements for products and services

question: Can someone sue a company for selling an inaccessible product?
answer: Yes. The EAA requires member states to ensure that consumer organisations can bring legal actions to enforce the rules. This means a disability rights group could take a company to court for placing an inaccessible product on the market. However, the Act does not provide for individual damages — it relies on market surveillance authorities to enforce compliance through orders to withdraw products or impose fines.
title: Directive (EU) 2019/882 on the accessibility requirements for products and services

question: Why did Europe pass this law instead of just relying on existing national rules?
answer: Because the patchwork of national laws was actually reducing accessibility. In the Commission's 2017 survey, 80% of companies reported that differing national requirements increased their compliance costs by 30-50%, making it cheaper to produce one inaccessible product for the whole EU than to adapt to 27 different standards. The EAA was designed to create a single, predictable set of rules, making it easier for companies to invest in accessibility — and easier for consumers to find accessible products across borders.
title: Directive (EU) 2019/882 on the accessibility requirements for products and services

question: Does EU law require my town's water utility to have a backup plan for a cyberattack?
answer: Yes, if the utility serves more than 10,000 people or is identified as "critical" by your member state. It must conduct a risk assessment, implement physical and cybersecurity measures, and report significant disruptions to national authorities.
title: Directive (EU) 2022/2557 on the resilience of critical entities

question: Why did the EU adopt separate laws for cybersecurity and physical resilience?
answer: The 2016 NIS Directive only covered cybersecurity for digital service providers. The CER Directive fills gaps for physical threats - like sabotage of power transformers or use of drones against pipelines - that could also cause cascading collapses across borders.
title: Directive (EU) 2022/2557 on the resilience of critical entities

question: Can a foreign government attack a hospital to disrupt elections?
answer: The directive explicitly covers health sector entities as potential targets of hybrid warfare. It requires member states to ensure hospitals have backup power and systems to maintain essential functions during an attack.
title: Directive (EU) 2022/2557 on the resilience of critical entities

question: What happens if my country doesn't implement this directive?
answer: The Commission can take member states to the Court of Justice of the EU. In 2024, Poland, Belgium, and Bulgaria faced infringement proceedings for failing to transpose the directive by the October deadline, risking daily fines.
title: Directive (EU) 2022/2557 on the resilience of critical entities

question: Why did the EU suddenly set much higher renewable energy targets in 2023?
answer: Russia's February 2022 invasion of Ukraine transformed renewable energy from a climate issue into a security imperative. With 40% of EU gas imports coming from Russia, the Kremlin's weaponisation of energy supplies caused prices to spike tenfold, threatening economic collapse. The EU's previous target of 32% had been set in 2018 during normal geopolitical conditions; the 2023 crisis made a dramatic increase politically unavoidable.
title: Directive (EU) 2023/2413 on the promotion of energy from renewable sources (RED III)

question: How fast do renewable projects need to get built under the new rules?
answer: The directive mandates maximum permitting times of 12 months for projects in designated "go-to areas" and 24 months elsewhere. Before this, permitting delays of 4-7 years were common across the EU. Projects are also declared "overriding public interest," which limits legal challenges. Member states must identify go-to areas by 2025, and the Commission reported in early 2024 that several countries had already cut permitting times by more than half.
title: Directive (EU) 2023/2413 on the promotion of energy from renewable sources (RED III)

question: Does every EU country have to meet the same renewable target individually?
answer: No. The 42.5% target is binding for the EU as a whole, but each member state has a negotiated individual contribution based on its GDP, renewable potential, and starting point. Wealthier states like Sweden (already at over 60% renewables) have higher targets, while coal-dependent states like Poland have lower ones. However, the Commission can enforce compliance through infringement proceedings if the collective trajectory is missed.
title: Directive (EU) 2023/2413 on the promotion of energy from renewable sources (RED III)

question: Can countries ignore the fast-track permitting rules if they don't want more renewables?
answer: No. The permitting acceleration measures are mandatory for all member states. If a country fails to designate "go-to areas" or fails to meet the 12/24-month deadlines, the Commission can open infringement proceedings. By early 2024, the Commission was already issuing letters of formal notice to several member states that had not begun transposition. The Council's legal service confirmed in a 2023 opinion that the permitting rules are directly binding.
title: Directive (EU) 2023/2413 on the promotion of energy from renewable sources (RED III)

question: Why did the new EU building rules target only the worst-performing 16% of offices and shops instead of all inefficient ones?
answer: The 16% threshold is a political compromise between the Commission's original proposal to target 15% of the worst-performing non-residential stock, and the European Parliament's call for a more aggressive 20-25% target. This figure was chosen because data from EU countries showed that the worst-performing 15-20% of buildings account for roughly 40-50% of total building energy consumption, according to the Commission's 2021 impact assessment. More aggressive targets were opposed by the governments of Germany, Poland, and Hungary, who argued that mandatory renovation of the entire inefficient stock would be too costly and socially disruptive.
title: Directive (EU) 2024/1275 on the Energy Performance of Buildings (recast)

question: Why will EU rules phase out fossil fuel boilers by 2040 rather than sooner?
answer: The 2040 phase-out date reflects a political compromise between the Commission's original proposal of 2035 (later accelerated to 2029 under REPowerEU), the European Parliament's preferred 2028 date, and the Council's resistance to any mandatory phase-out. The European Heating Industry association lobbied heavily for a "technology-open" approach, arguing that hydrogen-ready boilers and hybrid heat pumps would be more cost-effective for households in poorly insulated buildings. The final text allows limited exemptions for hybrid systems (fossil fuel boilers paired with heat pumps) and for Member States with cold climates, as requested by Finland and Sweden.
title: Directive (EU) 2024/1275 on the Energy Performance of Buildings (recast)

question: Why did the EU set only "suggested" energy-saving goals for homes instead of mandatory ones?
answer: This was the core political disagreement between the European Parliament and the Council, resolved through the "German compromise" in December 2023. The German government, which had initially supported binding targets under Minister Robert Habeck, shifted its position after a constitutional court ruling in November 2023 that limited the government's ability to use off-budget funds for renovation subsidies. The compromise meant moving from individual building obligations to national trajectories, which gave Member States flexibility to design their own mix of incentives, subsidies, and regulations. Belgium, the Netherlands, and Denmark opposed the softening, arguing that without binding targets, national governments would delay implementation — as they had with the previous 2018 directive's voluntary renovation strategies.
title: Directive (EU) 2024/1275 on the Energy Performance of Buildings (recast)

question: How do the new building efficiency rules work alongside the EU's carbon pricing system?
answer: The directive is designed to complement the revised Emissions Trading System (ETS), which will cover building emissions from 2027. Under the ETS2, fuel suppliers will need to purchase allowances for heating fuels, raising energy costs for consumers. The EPBD's renovation requirements and the Social Climate Fund are intended to mitigate the social impact of carbon pricing by helping households reduce energy demand before the ETS2 kicks in. However, the Commission's impact assessment warned that if renovation rates remain low, the combined effect of higher carbon costs and required renovations could create a "renovation poverty" trap for low-income households in poorly performing buildings.
title: Directive (EU) 2024/1275 on the Energy Performance of Buildings (recast)

question: Does every EU country have to create a plan to renovate old buildings?
answer: Yes. Every member state must adopt a national Building Renovation Roadmap by 2026, with legally binding intermediate targets for 2030, 2040, and 2050. These roadmaps must show how the country will transform its building stock to zero-emission standards, and the European Commission can launch infringement proceedings if a roadmap is insufficient or missed.
title: Directive (EU) 2024/1275 on the energy performance of buildings (recast)

question: When will gas and oil boilers be banned in the EU?
answer: The directive requires EU countries to phase out the use of fossil fuel boilers in buildings by 2040. From 2025, member states must stop providing subsidies for installing stand-alone fossil fuel boilers. After 2040, no new or replacement fossil fuel boiler can be sold or installed. However, hybrid heat pump systems can still include a backup boiler if necessary.
title: Directive (EU) 2024/1275 on the energy performance of buildings (recast)

question: Do new buildings have to produce their own energy?
answer: From 2030, all new buildings must be zero-emission, meaning their very low energy demand must be fully covered by renewable sources (typically rooftop solar or heat pumps). For public buildings, this applies from 2028. Additionally, solar energy installations are mandatory on all new public and commercial buildings from 2027, and on new residential buildings from 2030.
title: Directive (EU) 2024/1275 on the energy performance of buildings (recast)

question: How will poorer households afford renovations?
answer: The directive is linked to the Social Climate Fund (Regulation (EU) 2023/955), which has an estimated €3 billion available for 2025-2027 to support low-income households, micro-enterprises, and transport users affected by the transition. Member states can also use cohesion funds and the Recovery and Resilience Facility. Governments are required to include measures in their roadmaps to prevent energy poverty, such as subsidies for deep renovation and heat pump installation.
title: Directive (EU) 2024/1275 on the energy performance of buildings (recast)

question: Can someone sue an AI company for damages in the EU?
answer: Yes, as of the 2024 recast. If an AI system injures someone — by crashing a self-driving car, misdiagnosing an illness, or causing a fire through a faulty update — the manufacturer is strictly liable. The victim does not need to prove the manufacturer was negligent, only that the AI was defective and the defect caused the harm. For self-learning AI that changes after sale, liability remains with the original developer.
title: Directive (EU) 2024/2853 on Liability for Defective Products — software and AI now covered by the strict liability rules

question: Why does the EU now treat software like a toaster?
answer: The 1985 Product Liability Directive was created for physical goods. But by 2023, most consumer products had embedded software, and many had no hardware defect — the code was the problem. The 2024 recast simply updates the law to reflect reality: a defective algorithm that causes injury is as dangerous as a faulty brake pedal. Courts and companies need the same rules for both.
title: Directive (EU) 2024/2853 on Liability for Defective Products — software and AI now covered by the strict liability rules

question: Is an open-source developer liable if their code causes harm?
answer: It depends. If the developer places the software on the EU market as a "product" — by selling or distributing it commercially — they are strictly liable. But if they provide code for free on platforms like GitHub, with no commercial relationship to the user, the directive's liability regime does not apply. However, if the open-source component is integrated into a commercial product sold in the EU, the commercial manufacturer bears the liability.
title: Directive (EU) 2024/2853 on Liability for Defective Products — software and AI now covered by the strict liability rules

question: How does this interact with the EU AI Act?
answer: The AI Act (2024/1689/EU) sets safety requirements for high-risk AI systems, including risk management and documentation obligations. The Product Liability recast provides the legal remedy: if an AI system meets the AI Act's standards but still causes harm, the victim can still sue for damages. The two laws work together — the AI Act prevents harm, the liability directive compensates for it.
title: Directive (EU) 2024/2853 on Liability for Defective Products — software and AI now covered by the strict liability rules

question: Can someone sue an AI company for damages in the EU?
answer: Yes, under Directive 2024/2853, a person harmed by a defective AI system — such as an autonomous car crash, a faulty medical diagnosis, or a chatbot that gives dangerous advice — can sue the developer or manufacturer without proving fault. The claimant only needs to show that the AI was defective (failed to meet safety requirements) and that the defect caused the damage. For AI "black boxes" whose decision-making is opaque, the burden shifts to the company to prove it wasn't defective.
title: Directive (EU) 2024/2853 on liability for defective products — software and AI now covered by strict liability rules

question: Does EU law cover software updates that break my phone or car?
answer: Yes. The directive explicitly treats software updates, including over-the-air updates, as part of the product. If an update introduces a defect — such as disabling safety features in a car or bricking a medical device — the manufacturer is liable even if the original hardware was fine. This closes the loophole that previously allowed companies to disclaim liability by calling software "services" rather than "products."
title: Directive (EU) 2024/2853 on liability for defective products — software and AI now covered by strict liability rules

question: Why does the EU hold companies liable for software but the US does not?
answer: The EU's strict liability regime is broader than US product liability law, which generally requires proving negligence or a specific design defect. The EU approach, based on the principle of consumer protection, reflects a different legal tradition: the 1985 directive was rooted in German and French civil law systems that prioritise consumer safety over freedom of contract. The 2024 revision extends this principle to the digital age, whereas the US has maintained a more cautious approach, partly because of industry lobbying and fears of stifling innovation. The EU's move was also motivated by the realisation that US-style litigation against AI companies — which rarely succeeds — leaves European consumers with no remedy.
title: Directive (EU) 2024/2853 on liability for defective products — software and AI now covered by strict liability rules

question: What damage can be claimed under the new rules?
answer: Consumers can claim for three types of damage: (1) death or personal injury, (2) damage to property (including digital assets like data loss, but only if the data is lost because of product defect), and (3) non-material damage recognised by national law — such as psychological trauma from a defective AI, or privacy loss. This is a major expansion from the 1985 directive, which only covered physical injury and property damage. The Commission estimated that non-material damage claims could increase by 30–50% in the first five years of the new regime.
title: Directive (EU) 2024/2853 on liability for defective products — software and AI now covered by strict liability rules

question: Why did the EU need to make rules for resolving consumer complaints outside court instead of just asking businesses to do it voluntarily?
answer: Voluntary ADR existed in many member states before 2013, but coverage was patchy and quality varied enormously. In the 2011 impact assessment, the Commission found that only Belgium and the Netherlands had comprehensive ADR coverage; Spain, Greece, and several Eastern European countries had none. Without a directive imposing minimum standards, a trader in one country could offer a poor-quality ADR body that would undermine consumer trust across borders. The directive ensured that every member state had at least one quality-assured ADR body for every sector, making the ODR platform—which routes cases to these bodies—feasible.
title: Directive on Alternative Dispute Resolution for Consumer Disputes

question: Why don't EU rules force businesses to take part in out-of-court complaint resolution for consumers?
answer: The directive operates on an "opt-in" model: traders must inform consumers about ADR options, but are not required to use them. This was a deliberate political compromise to avoid ECJ challenges (following the *Caja de Ahorros* ruling) and to placate business groups, who argued that mandatory participation would impose disproportionate costs on SMEs. However, some member states—notably the Netherlands and Germany—have gone further, making ADR mandatory for specific sectors like energy and telecoms under national law. The EU's 2019 evaluation recommended against a mandatory EU-wide rule, but left the door open for national-level mandates.
title: Directive on Alternative Dispute Resolution for Consumer Disputes

question: What is the difference between resolving a consumer complaint through a mediator and doing it entirely online?
answer: The ADR Directive (2013/11/EU) creates the national infrastructure of impartial dispute-resolution bodies. The ODR Regulation (524/2013/EU) creates the digital interface—the consumer-facing platform where a buyer in one country can file a complaint online and have it automatically routed to the correct national ADR body. The ODR platform launched in February 2016; by 2023, it had processed over 1.2 million complaints (Commission ODR platform report). The two laws are designed as a pair: without the ADR Directive ensuring bodies exist, the ODR platform would have no destination for cases; without the ODR platform, cross-border consumers would have difficulty finding the right ADR body.
title: Directive on Alternative Dispute Resolution for Consumer Disputes

question: Does every EU country have to offer free dispute resolution for consumer complaints?
answer: Yes, but the service isn't always free. The directive requires each member state to ensure there are ADR entities covering all commercial sectors, and many of these are free or low-cost for consumers. However, the directive allows ADR bodies to charge fees, as long as they remain affordable. In practice, most consumer ADR in the EU is either free or capped at €10-20, though complex cases in some countries can cost up to €100.
title: Directive on Alternative Dispute Resolution for Consumer Disputes

question: Can a company force a customer to go to arbitration instead of court?
answer: No, not for consumer disputes. The 2016 Menini v Banco Popolare ruling made clear that ADR agreements in consumer contracts are not binding — the consumer must always retain the right to take the case to court. This overturned earlier interpretations where some traders included mandatory arbitration clauses. The directive protects the consumer's freedom to choose.
title: Directive on Alternative Dispute Resolution for Consumer Disputes

question: Why did so many EU countries miss the deadline to set up dispute resolution systems?
answer: The short two-year transposition deadline caught many governments off guard. Setting up ADR bodies requires legislative changes, regulations for quality standards, and often new agencies with trained mediators. Countries like Poland, Slovakia, and Romania cited "administrative capacity issues" and "coordination problems between ministries." The Commission admitted in its 2018 report that the deadline was "highly optimistic" and noted that smaller member states struggled most because they lacked existing ADR infrastructure.
title: Directive on Alternative Dispute Resolution for Consumer Disputes

question: What happens if a trader ignores an ADR decision?
answer: The directive does not make ADR decisions binding on traders unless the trader has agreed to be bound. This was a deliberate compromise — BusinessEurope and national governments insisted on voluntary participation for businesses. Consumers cannot enforce a non-binding ADR decision in court; they can only use the decision as evidence. However, traders who refuse to participate in ADR face reputational pressure: the ADR body must publish information on non-cooperative traders, and the trader must also state clearly on their website that they do not use ADR.
title: Directive on Alternative Dispute Resolution for Consumer Disputes

question: Does every EU country have a free way to resolve a dispute with a shop without going to court?
answer: Yes, the ADR Directive requires every EU Member State to ensure at least one independent, certified body exists that can handle consumer complaints out of court. In most countries, the service is free or low-cost. The European Consumer Centre Network (ECC-Net) can tell you which body to contact for your specific issue and country.
title: Directive on Alternative Dispute Resolution for Consumer Disputes (2013/11/EU)

question: Can a retailer refuse to use an alternative dispute resolution service if one exists?
answer: Yes, in most sectors. The ADR Directive does not force traders to participate in ADR—it only requires that Member States *provide* the infrastructure. However, since 2019, the Commission has been pushing for mandatory participation in sectors where the directive has been most ineffective, particularly in e-commerce and digital services.
title: Directive on Alternative Dispute Resolution for Consumer Disputes (2013/11/EU)

question: Why did it take the EU so long to pass consumer ADR rules?
answer: Because earlier attempts (the 2008 Mediation Directive) were voluntary and left Member States free to decide whether to fund ADR bodies. The 2008–2011 financial crisis changed the calculus: national courts were overwhelmed with consumer credit and mortgage cases, and governments saw ADR as a cheap way to clear backlogs. The 2011 Eurobarometer survey also showed that traders were losing cross-border sales because consumers didn't trust they could resolve complaints cheaply, breaking the business opposition.
title: Directive on Alternative Dispute Resolution for Consumer Disputes (2013/11/EU)

question: Is the ADR Directive still relevant for online disputes today?
answer: Yes, but it is about to be updated. The 2023 Commission proposal (COM/2023/647) will make ADR mandatory for traders in certain sectors (like airlines, telecoms, and digital platforms) and will require platforms like Amazon to provide clear ADR links in their checkout process. The current directive's biggest weakness—voluntary participation by traders—is being eliminated for the most frequent dispute categories.
title: Directive on Alternative Dispute Resolution for Consumer Disputes (2013/11/EU)

question: Can I use any government data for free in the EU?
answer: Not all data, but high-value datasets in six categories (geospatial, earth observation, meteorological, statistical, company ownership, and transport) must be free and available via API by June 2024. Other public sector data can still have fees, but only at marginal cost of reproduction — usually no more than a few euros per dataset. Exclusive deals where one company gets special access are banned.
title: Directive on open data and the re-use of public sector information (recast)

question: Why did EU rules on public data take so long to update?
answer: The original 2003 directive let public bodies charge fees that covered collection and dissemination costs — which national mapping agencies and weather services used to fund their operations. The change happened because independent studies showed the economic value of free data far exceeded the fee revenue lost, and because data-driven startups convinced national finance ministries that open data was cheaper than trying to build an AI industry with locked-up public information.
title: Directive on open data and the re-use of public sector information (recast)

question: What counts as a high-value dataset under this directive?
answer: The European Commission defined six categories: geospatial data (addresses, buildings, land parcels), earth observation data (Copernicus satellite data), meteorological data (temperature, precipitation, wind), statistical data (GDP, population, employment), company and company ownership data (business registers), and transport data (traffic flow, public transport schedules, road works). These must be free of charge, machine-readable, and available through APIs that allow real-time access.
title: Directive on open data and the re-use of public sector information (recast)

question: Can a city government charge for its open data if it needs the money?
answer: No — for high-value datasets, charging is banned. For other public sector information, public bodies can charge at marginal cost (the cost of reproduction and dissemination), but not more. If a public body needs to recover full costs, it must apply for a specific derogation from the Commission — which has only been granted twice, both for specialised research data collections from the European Library.
title: Directive on open data and the re-use of public sector information (recast)

question: Which companies in Europe are required to have cybersecurity measures?
answer: The NIS Directive applied to two groups. "Operators of essential services" in sectors like energy, transport, banking, health, and water — any company that a Member State designated as critical to providing essential services. "Digital service providers" like cloud platforms, search engines, and online marketplaces were also covered but with lighter obligations. The crucial detail: each Member State decided its own list of essential operators, leading to huge variation — a hospital cluster might be regulated in one country but not the next.
title: Directive on security of network and information systems (NIS Directive)

question: Why did Europe's first cybersecurity law take three years to pass?
answer: The main obstacle was national sovereignty. Governments argued security classifications should be their own business, and some (especially the UK and Poland) opposed any EU-level mandate to share incident data. The deadlock broke only after real-world cyberattacks — the 2015 Ukraine power grid attack and the 2016 Bangladesh Bank heist — showed that threats routinely crossed borders and that no country could protect critical infrastructure alone.
title: Directive on security of network and information systems (NIS Directive)

question: Does the NIS Directive still apply today?
answer: No. It was replaced by the NIS 2 Directive (2022/2555/EU), which entered into force in January 2023. NIS 2 expanded the scope to more sectors (including public administration, space, and food processing), abolished the two-tier system, and introduced much tougher enforcement with fines up to €10 million or 2% of global turnover. The original NIS Directive was in force from 2016 to 2023, with Member States required to comply from May 2018.
title: Directive on security of network and information systems (NIS Directive)

question: What happened to companies that didn't comply with the NIS Directive?
answer: Enforcement was inconsistent because each Member State set its own penalties. The Commission reported in 2020 that some countries had no fines at all, others had procedures that took years. The most common consequence was regulatory pressure and reputational harm rather than financial penalty. This weak enforcement was a key reason the NIS 2 Directive introduced mandatory EU-minimum fines and personal liability for company directors.
title: Directive on security of network and information systems (NIS Directive)

question: Can the EU's critical infrastructure be hacked or sabotaged by a foreign power?
answer: Yes, and the CER Directive is a direct response to that growing threat. The 2015-2016 cyberattacks on Ukraine's power grid, which caused physical blackouts, showed that critical infrastructure can be disabled remotely. The directive requires operators of energy grids, hospitals, water systems, and transport hubs to conduct risk assessments and implement security measures—though enforcement varies widely by country. It stops short of common EU-level certification or mandatory penetration testing, leaving that to member states.
title: Directive on the resilience of critical entities (CER Directive)

question: Does the EU require hospitals and power plants to protect against terrorist attacks?
answer: Not directly—the CER Directive requires each member state to identify "critical entities" and for those entities to take "proportionate" security measures. It does not mandate specific protections like armed guards or blast-proof walls, leaving the choice of measures up to the entity (e.g., access controls, video surveillance, backup systems). After the 2015 Paris attacks, France unilaterally imposed stricter requirements on its power plants; other countries like Malta took no action and now face implementation deadlines by 2026.
title: Directive on the resilience of critical entities (CER Directive)

question: Why did it take until 2022 to update the old critical infrastructure rules?
answer: The original 2008 ECI Directive was adopted as a terrorism-only measure after the 2004 Madrid and 2005 London bombings. It took Russia's 2014 annexation of Crimea, the 2015-2016 Ukraine grid hacks, and the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic to prove that the threats had expanded—cyberattacks, natural disasters, and hybrid warfare. The 2019 Commission evaluation showed that most member states had simply ignored the old directive. The 2021 Colonial Pipeline attack in the U.S. broke the final deadlock, as it made the immediate risk of remote digital sabotage real to European governments.
title: Directive on the resilience of critical entities (CER Directive)

question: Can a private company be forced to share incident reports with EU authorities?
answer: Under the CER Directive, yes—there is a mandatory obligation to report any incident that significantly disrupts the provision of a critical service. The report must be made to the national competent authority within 24 hours for major incidents (e.g., a hospital ransomware attack that halts surgeries) and within 72 hours for lesser disruptions. The authority may share the report with other member states if the incident has cross-border effects. However, the directive allows limited protection: authorities can keep reports confidential if their disclosure would expose commercial secrets.
title: Directive on the resilience of critical entities (CER Directive)

question: Can an employer in the EU refuse to hire someone because of their religion?
answer: No, but there is a narrow exception. An employer can require a specific religion or belief only if it is a "genuine and determining occupational requirement" — typically for religious organisations hiring clergy, chaplains, or religious teachers. Even then, the requirement must be proportionate and justified by the nature of the job. The European Court of Justice has strictly limited this exception, ruling in *Egenberger* (2018) that national courts must verify that any such requirement is genuinely necessary.
title: Employment Equality Directive (2000/78/EC) — equal treatment framework for religion, disability, age and sexual orientation

question: Does EU law require employers to make workplaces accessible for employees with disabilities?
answer: Yes. The directive requires "reasonable accommodation" for disabled workers — meaning employers must take appropriate measures to enable a qualified person with a disability to do the job, unless this would impose a "disproportionate burden" on the employer. This includes modifying workstations, adjusting working hours, providing assistive technology, or assigning different duties. What qualifies as "disproportionate" is assessed case-by-case, considering the employer's resources, available public funding, and the accommodation's cost. Many member states provide subsidies to help employers cover these costs.
title: Employment Equality Directive (2000/78/EC) — equal treatment framework for religion, disability, age and sexual orientation

question: Why can employers still set mandatory retirement ages under EU law?
answer: The directive explicitly permits differences of treatment on grounds of age if they are "objectively and reasonably justified by a legitimate aim" — such as promoting youth employment, ensuring workforce planning, or maintaining intergenerational fairness. The European Court of Justice has upheld some national mandatory retirement ages (e.g., Spain's compulsory retirement at 65 for judges in *Petersen*, 2010), while striking down others that it found disproportionate (e.g., Austria's automatic termination of employment contracts at age 65 in *Österreichischer Gewerkschaftsbund*, 2018). Member states have wide discretion, but the law must be justified by a genuine policy aim.
title: Employment Equality Directive (2000/78/EC) — equal treatment framework for religion, disability, age and sexual orientation

question: Does the directive cover discrimination based on sexual orientation outside of employment?
answer: No — not directly. The Employment Equality Directive only covers employment, occupation, vocational training, and membership of professional organisations. Discrimination in access to goods and services (e.g., housing, healthcare, education) based on sexual orientation is not covered by this directive. However, the 2008 Commission proposal for a broader "Equal Treatment Directive" (COM(2008) 426) would have extended protection to all four grounds (religion, disability, age, sexual orientation) beyond employment, but it has been blocked in the Council since 2008 due to opposition from several member states, notably Germany (under Chancellor Angela Merkel) and Poland.
title: Employment Equality Directive (2000/78/EC) — equal treatment framework for religion, disability, age and sexual orientation

question: Can employers in the EU still require employees to follow a specific religion?
answer: Yes, but only in limited circumstances. The directive allows churches and other religious organisations to require employees to act in good faith with their ethos — for example, a Catholic school can require a religious education teacher to be Catholic. But this exemption only applies where the religion or belief is a genuine, legitimate, and justified occupational requirement, proportionate to the job's nature. An accountant working for a church-affiliated charity, for instance, could not be dismissed for being an atheist.
title: Employment Equality Directive (2000/78/EC)

question: Why did the EU ban age discrimination in hiring but allow retirement ages to remain?
answer: The directive explicitly permits differences of treatment on grounds of age where they are "objectively and reasonably justified by a legitimate aim" — including employment policy, labour market, and vocational training objectives. This created legal space for mandatory retirement ages, early retirement schemes, and age-based pay scales, provided they serve a legitimate social policy goal. The *Mangold* case (2005) confirmed this, but also established that any age-based distinction must be proportionate and necessary.
title: Employment Equality Directive (2000/78/EC)

question: What counts as "reasonable accommodation" for a disabled employee?
answer: Reasonable accommodation means adjustments to the workplace, working hours, or working arrangements that allow a disabled person to perform their job — such as providing voice-to-text software for a deaf telemarketer, installing a ramp for a wheelchair user, or allowing flexible hours for someone with a chronic condition. Employers are not required to make adjustments that impose a "disproportionate burden," considering factors like the employer's size, financial resources, and the cost of the adjustment. Small businesses with fewer than 10 employees are generally not expected to make major structural changes.
title: Employment Equality Directive (2000/78/EC)

question: Why did the directive take so long to pass, and why has it not been updated since 2000?
answer: The directive took three years from the Treaty of Amsterdam (1997) to adoption because of fierce opposition from several member states — particularly Austria, Germany, and Ireland — combined with the need to agree on a single text covering four different grounds of discrimination. Since 2000, the Commission has tried to extend the protections to social protection, healthcare, and education (the "horizontal directive" proposed in 2010), but this has been blocked in the Council by countries including Germany (under the Merkel government), the Netherlands, and several eastern member states, who argue it would interfere with national welfare systems and the operation of faith-based schools.
title: Employment Equality Directive (2000/78/EC)

question: Why did the EU suddenly require member states to cut energy use by 11.7%?
answer: The 11.7% target emerged from the Commission's 2021 modelling showing that existing voluntary approaches were insufficient to meet the EU's legally binding 55% emissions cut by 2030. The Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022 transformed this technical goal into a geopolitical necessity: reducing energy consumption directly cut demand for Russian gas. The target was calculated against the 2020 reference scenario, not actual 2020 consumption, to avoid penalising countries that had already improved efficiency.
title: Energy Efficiency Directive recast (2023/1791/EU) — binding efficiency-first obligations

question: Does every company in the EU now need an energy audit?
answer: No. Only "large enterprises" — defined as companies with over 250 employees and annual turnover above €50 million — must implement an energy management system and conduct regular audits. SMEs (small and medium-sized enterprises) are exempt from mandatory audits, though member states are encouraged to provide them with free or subsidised energy audits. The directive's annual savings obligations fall on member states, not individual businesses, though states can (and do) pass the burden onto larger companies via national legislation.
title: Energy Efficiency Directive recast (2023/1791/EU) — binding efficiency-first obligations

question: How can the EU enforce the "energy efficiency first" principle?
answer: The "efficiency first" principle is now a legally binding obligation, not just a political slogan. Member states must demonstrate in their NECPs how they prioritised energy efficiency over supply-side investments in major infrastructure decisions. If a country builds a new power plant without showing it studied efficiency alternatives first, the Commission could launch infringement proceedings at the Court of Justice of the EU. However, the principle is procedural, not absolute — a country can still build a gas plant if it can prove efficiency measures were infeasible. The first test case came in 2023 when Poland's plans for new gas units faced Commission scrutiny on these grounds.
title: Energy Efficiency Directive recast (2023/1791/EU) — binding efficiency-first obligations

question: What happens if a country doesn't meet its 1.49% annual savings target?
answer: Member states face the full enforcement machinery of EU law. If the Commission determines a country is falling short, it can issue a formal notice, followed by a reasoned opinion, and ultimately refer the case to the Court of Justice of the EU. The Court can impose lump-sum fines or daily penalty payments — typically ranging from €10,000 to over €100,000 per day, calculated based on the country's GDP and voting weight. In theory, repeated non-compliance could lead to suspension of EU funds, though this has never been applied. The real enforcement pressure comes from the reporting cycle: countries that miss their 2026 interim targets face immediate Commission scrutiny.
title: Energy Efficiency Directive recast (2023/1791/EU) — binding efficiency-first obligations

question: Does EU law force countries to save a specific amount of energy every year?
answer: Yes. The Energy Efficiency Directive sets a binding target for the EU as a whole to reduce final energy consumption by 11.7% by 2030 compared to 2020 projections. Each member state must contribute through its National Energy and Climate Plan, though the law allows for flexibility: if the EU as a whole is off-track, the Commission can require individual countries to increase their contributions. The law also requires each country to achieve new annual energy savings of 1.5% of final energy consumption from 2025 through 2030.
title: Energy Efficiency Directive recast (2023/1791/EU) — binding efficiency-first obligations

question: What does "energy efficiency first" mean in practice?
answer: It means that when making investment, policy, or planning decisions, public authorities must consider energy efficiency alternatives before building new energy supply or infrastructure. For example, before approving a new gas power plant, a national regulator must assess whether demand-side measures like building insulation or industrial efficiency upgrades could achieve the same results at lower cost. This principle was previously voluntary; the recast makes it a binding legal requirement in all energy-related decisions.
title: Energy Efficiency Directive recast (2023/1791/EU) — binding efficiency-first obligations

question: Are companies in Europe forced to monitor their energy use?
answer: Large companies are. The directive requires any company with an average annual energy consumption above a threshold (the Commission is to define this by December 2024) to implement an energy management system certified to European standards such as ISO 50001. This covers energy audits, monitoring, and reduction targets in manufacturing, logistics, and buildings. Companies below the threshold are still subject to mandatory energy audits at least every four years.
title: Energy Efficiency Directive recast (2023/1791/EU) — binding efficiency-first obligations

question: Can the EU fine a country for not saving enough energy?
answer: Yes, potentially. The 11.7% target is legally binding at the EU level. If a member state fails to include adequate measures in its National Energy and Climate Plan, or fails to implement those measures, the European Commission can open infringement proceedings under Article 258 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the EU. This could ultimately lead to the Court of Justice of the EU imposing fines. However, the target is collective rather than individually binding on each country, meaning the Commission focuses on assessing and correcting national contributions rather than fining countries solely for missing the target.
title: Energy Efficiency Directive recast (2023/1791/EU) — binding efficiency-first obligations

question: Why did EU energy-saving rules switch from setting targets for each country to one overall target for the whole EU?
answer: The shift was a political compromise. Germany and France argued during Council negotiations in late 2022 that setting binding national targets was too rigid given the energy crisis and volatile forecasts. They preferred indicative national contributions with a binding EU overall target — giving them flexibility to adjust through national plans while still holding the bloc collectively accountable. This model mirrors the approach used under the Effort Sharing Regulation.
title: Energy Efficiency Directive (Recast)

question: Why are EU countries now required to save almost twice as much energy each year (1.49%) compared to before?
answer: The Commission's 2021 impact assessment modelled that achieving the original 32.5% target required a 0.8% savings rate, but reaching the new 55% emissions goal demanded deeper cuts. Commissioner Kadri Simson stated in the 14 July 2021 press conference that "efficiency is the hidden fuel of the energy transition." The 1.49% figure came from a linear interpolation between the 0.8% rate and what the Commission's modelling showed was needed to stay on the 1.5°C pathway under the Paris Agreement. The European Parliament's ITRE committee had pushed for 1.5%, settling on 1.49% during trilogue talks in March 2023.
title: Energy Efficiency Directive (Recast)

question: How do the EU's energy-saving rules work together with the rules on making buildings more energy efficient?
answer: The EED 2023 sets horizontal efficiency obligations across all sectors, while the EPBD (2024/1275/EU) specifically governs building performance. The EED's 3% renovation rate for public buildings complements the EPBD's new requirement for zero-emission buildings from 2030. Both directives now share the same timeline for national transposition (October 2025), and member states are expected to submit joint implementation plans. The Commission's 2023 guidance document notes that overlap exists — for instance, public building renovations required under the EED must meet the EPBD's energy performance standards.
title: Energy Efficiency Directive (Recast)

question: Do I have to renovate my home because of EU climate rules?
answer: Yes, indirectly. The directive requires each EU country to adopt a national renovation roadmap that will set targets to reduce average energy use in residential buildings — from 2033, average primary energy use must drop by 16%, and by 20-22% by 2035. This means individual homeowners are not directly fined, but Member States will design national policies (subsidies, loans, possibly minimum standards at sale or rental point) to meet these targets. If you live in a very energy-inefficient home (e.g., an uninsulated 1960s apartment block), you will likely face renovation requirements when you try to sell or rent it after around 2030-2033, depending on your country's implementation.
title: Energy Performance of Buildings Directive (recast 2024/1275/EU) — zero-emission buildings and renovation roadmaps

question: When do fossil fuel heating systems have to be replaced in the EU?
answer: From 2025, EU countries cannot give subsidies for stand-alone fossil fuel boilers (though hybrid systems combining a heat pump with a boiler can still receive support until 2030). The end date for operating fossil fuel boilers in buildings is 2040 — by that year, the entire building stock must be fossil-fuel-free. This does not require ripping out a perfectly working gas boiler before 2040, but any replacement after 2025 will likely need to be a non-fossil fuel system (heat pump, district heating, solar thermal, or hydrogen-ready under specific conditions). The exact national phase-out date may vary: Germany's coalition has already set 2028 for new boiler installations in existing homes, while Italy has yet to announce a specific date.
title: Energy Performance of Buildings Directive (recast 2024/1275/EU) — zero-emission buildings and renovation roadmaps

question: Will EU rules make me pay for climate certification of my building?
answer: Not directly, but they will affect the value of your property and your renovation costs. The directive strengthens energy performance certificates (EPCs): from around 2030, tenants and buyers must be shown the EPC rating at the first property viewing, not just in the contract. This is already leading to "green mortgage" products from banks like ING and BNP Paribas, which offer lower interest rates for high-rated homes, while some lenders (including Deutsche Bank in 2023 pilot reports) are starting to discount the mortgage value of homes rated D or worse — the "brown discount" phenomenon.
title: Energy Performance of Buildings Directive (recast 2024/1275/EU) — zero-emission buildings and renovation roadmaps

question: Why isn't Europe just banning gas boilers immediately if it's so urgent?
answer: The main reason is affordability.
