The Commission's own impact assessment estimated that the total cost of renovating the EU building stock to zero-emission standard is roughly €1 trillion per year until 2050. An immediate ban on gas boilers in 2025 would have meant millions of households, especially in Central and Eastern Europe where many still rely on gas heating and have low incomes, facing replacement costs of €10,000-€20,000 per home at a time of high inflation. The phased approach — stopping subsidies in 2025, phasing out by 2040 — gives national governments and industries time to build heat pump manufacturing capacity, train installers (there are currently estimated 150,000 heat pump installers in the EU, but 1 million will be needed by 2030 per the Commission's Renovation Wave plan), and provide social support through the Social Climate Fund.
title: Energy Performance of Buildings Directive (recast 2024/1275/EU) — zero-emission buildings and renovation roadmaps

question: Why are religious discrimination claims in the workplace treated differently from other types of discrimination under EU employment rules?
answer: The directive permits religious organisations to require employees to respect their ethos, provided the requirement is genuine, legitimate, and proportionate. This was a political compromise to secure the directive's passage in 2000, as several member states (Ireland, Poland, Italy, and Germany) had strong church interests demanding that faith-based employers (schools, hospitals, charities) retain the ability to hire co-religionists. Without this exemption, the directive would have faced a blocking minority in the Council.
title: Equal Treatment in Employment Directive

question: Why weren't EU workplace anti-discrimination rules expanded to also cover things like buying a house or getting a service?
answer: A proposed "horizontal" directive covering access to goods and services (COM(2008) 426) was presented by Commissioner Vladimír Špidla in July 2008, but it stalled in the Council due to disagreements about the scope for services (healthcare, education, housing) and the treatment of multiple discrimination. As of 2025, the proposal remains blocked in the Council — 12 member states, led by Germany and Poland, have opposed it, arguing that it infringes national competences over social services and religious freedom.
title: Equal Treatment in Employment Directive

question: Why did it take so long for EU rules against workplace discrimination to actually be enforced?
answer: The directive allowed a three-year extension for the disability and age provisions, meaning that for some member states (Ireland, Luxembourg, Austria, and the UK requested and received extensions), national implementation deadlines fell in 2006. This gave member states with weaker social protection systems time to adjust. The Commission also faced a high-volume of complaints — by 2010, the Commission had received over 1,200 complaints related to age discrimination alone, according to its 2014 report on the application of the directive.
title: Equal Treatment in Employment Directive

question: How does Europe define a "green" investment?
answer: The EU Taxonomy sets six environmental goals and establishes technical screening criteria for economic activities to qualify as "environmentally sustainable." An activity must contribute substantially to at least one goal, do no significant harm to the other five (including social safeguards), and meet minimum labour and human rights protections. Companies and financial products that meet these criteria can legally claim their shares or investments are "taxonomy-aligned."
title: EU Taxonomy Regulation (2020/852/EU) — common definition of environmentally sustainable activity

question: Can fossil fuels like natural gas ever be considered "green" under EU rules?
answer: Yes, under limited and controversial conditions. The Taxonomy's Complementary Delegated Act classifies certain gas-fired power plants as "transitional" activities if they replace coal plants and meet strict emissions thresholds. Nuclear energy is also included under conditions related to waste management and safety upgrades. Environmental groups have sued the Commission over these inclusions, arguing they violate the "do no significant harm" principle.
title: EU Taxonomy Regulation (2020/852/EU) — common definition of environmentally sustainable activity

question: Does the Taxonomy apply to small businesses?
answer: Not directly. The disclosure obligations apply to large companies (over 500 employees) and financial market participants. However, small and medium-sized enterprises are indirectly affected because banks and investors ask them to provide data on whether their activities are taxonomy-aligned when assessing loan or investment eligibility. The EU is developing simplified reporting standards for SMEs, but as of early 2024, the burden remains a concern.
title: EU Taxonomy Regulation (2020/852/EU) — common definition of environmentally sustainable activity

question: Have any companies been penalised for breaking these rules?
answer: Yes. In May 2023, the Dutch regulator AFM fined asset manager Robeco €0.5 million for misleading sustainability claims that did not match their Taxonomy disclosures. By mid-2024, over 200 financial firms across the EU had received formal warnings from national regulators about non-compliant green marketing. No major criminal cases have been concluded yet, but the European Securities and Markets Authority conducts annual sweeps to identify greenwashing patterns.
title: EU Taxonomy Regulation (2020/852/EU) — common definition of environmentally sustainable activity

question: Is all investment labelled "green" in the EU actually sustainable?
answer: Not automatically. The Taxonomy Regulation does not mandate that products labelled "sustainable" must meet its criteria — it only requires disclosure of whether and how they align. Financial products can still be marketed as "green" using the regulator's own definitions. The European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) has repeatedly warned that firms may exaggerate alignment, and in 2023 it began targeted supervisory actions on asset managers' taxonomy disclosures.
title: EU Taxonomy Regulation — common definition of environmentally sustainable activity

question: Why does EU law allow gas and nuclear energy to be classified as sustainable?
answer: The 2022 delegated act on climate objectives included gas and nuclear under strict conditions — for example, gas plants must close by 2035 and emit less than 100g CO2 per kWh. This was a political compromise driven by France (nuclear) and central European countries (gas). The Commission's rationale was that excluding both would hinder certain member states' energy transition, even though many environmental groups fought fiercely against the decision.
title: EU Taxonomy Regulation — common definition of environmentally sustainable activity

question: Does the Taxonomy apply to both companies and financial institutions?
answer: Yes, but with different requirements. Large companies (over 500 employees) must disclose the proportion of their turnover, capital expenditure, and operating expenditure aligned with the taxonomy. Financial institutions — banks, insurers, pension funds — must disclose the taxonomy-alignment of their investment portfolios. Small and medium-sized enterprises are largely exempt unless they issue green bonds.
title: EU Taxonomy Regulation — common definition of environmentally sustainable activity

question: Can a company be sued for mismatching its green claims with the Taxonomy?
answer: Potentially, under the 2023 Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). The CSRD requires audit-level assurance on taxonomy disclosures. Misleading taxonomy reporting could trigger enforcement by national securities regulators or private lawsuits under EU consumer law, as seen in the 2022 "ClientEarth v. Shell" case where NGOs successfully argued that Shell's renewable energy claims were insufficiently substantiated.
title: EU Taxonomy Regulation — common definition of environmentally sustainable activity

question: Which digital products must be accessible in the EU?
answer: The law covers consumer computers, smartphones, tablets, e-readers, game consoles, smart TVs, and public-use devices like ATMs, ticket machines, and self-service check-in kiosks. It also includes banking services, e-commerce websites, e-books, and telecom services (voice calls, SMS). But it does not cover everything: for example, private websites, elevator buttons, or physical building spaces are excluded.
title: European Accessibility Act (2019/882/EU) — accessibility requirements for key digital products and services

question: Can an EU country set stricter accessibility rules than the EU law?
answer: Yes — but only if the product is fully produced and sold within that country. For cross-border trade, the EAA sets a maximum harmonisation ceiling: Member States cannot impose extra requirements on products legally sold across the EU. This is to prevent the old fragmentation. A few countries (like France and Italy) have tried, risking Commission infringement action.
title: European Accessibility Act (2019/882/EU) — accessibility requirements for key digital products and services

question: When do companies have to comply with the European Accessibility Act?
answer: The directive applies from June 28, 2025, for new products and services. There is a longer transition for services that were already in use before that date — they must comply by June 28, 2030. Micro-enterprises (fewer than 10 employees, turnover under €2 million) are exempt from some documentation obligations but still must make their products and services accessible.
title: European Accessibility Act (2019/882/EU) — accessibility requirements for key digital products and services

question: Is there a risk of non-compliance being ignored?
answer: Yes — enforcement is up to each Member State, and the Commission's 2023 report found that only 14 of 27 countries had fully transposed the law by then. The European Commission has opened infringement cases against six countries, but the real test will come in 2025-2026 when market surveillance authorities begin inspections. The European Disability Forum has warned that penalties vary widely — from €5,000 in some countries to €100,000 in Belgium — which could create a patchwork of enforcement.
title: European Accessibility Act (2019/882/EU) — accessibility requirements for key digital products and services

question: How fast can a country hand over a wanted person to another EU country?
answer: Under the European Arrest Warrant, a person who agrees to surrender must be handed over within 10 days of the judicial decision. If they contest the surrender, the process takes up to 90 days from the date of arrest. In practice, according to Eurojust, the average time is 16 days for agreed surrenders and 50 days for contested ones. This is a dramatic reduction from pre-2002 extradition, which often took 12 to 18 months.
title: European Arrest Warrant Framework Decision (2002/584/JHA)

question: Could a country refuse to send someone if their prison system is abusive?
answer: Yes, but only since 2018. In the *Aranyosi and Căldăraru* case, the European Court of Justice ruled that an executing judge can postpone surrender if there is evidence of systemic prison overcrowding or inhuman treatment in the requesting country. The judge must first ask the requesting country for guarantees about the person’s treatment. If no adequate guarantee is given, surrender can be refused temporarily. This was tragically tested in 2019 when a UK court refused to surrender a man to Hungary due to prison conditions.
title: European Arrest Warrant Framework Decision (2002/584/JHA)

question: Why did EU countries give up their right to veto extradition requests?
answer: After the 9/11 attacks, EU leaders feared that terrorists could hide in one country while planning attacks in another. The existing extradition system required a government minister to approve each request, which could take months or years and be blocked for political or diplomatic reasons. The European Arrest Warrant replaced this with a judge-to-judge system, removing politics from the process. The trade-off was speed and efficiency for trust: member states had to trust that each other’s legal systems met minimum standards of justice.
title: European Arrest Warrant Framework Decision (2002/584/JHA)

question: Who gets surrendered under the European Arrest Warrant?
answer: Anyone — from wanted terrorists to accused shoplifters. The EAW covers all crimes punishable by at least 12 months of imprisonment in the issuing country. For 32 categories of serious crimes (like terrorism, murder, rape, and fraud), the double criminality check — verifying the act is a crime in both countries — is abolished. This means someone can be surrendered for a crime that is illegal in the requesting country but legal in the surrendering country, as long as it falls on this list. Critics argue this violates national sovereignty.
title: European Arrest Warrant Framework Decision (2002/584/JHA)

question: Can a company sell a bond as "green" in the EU without it being a European Green Bond?
answer: Yes. The regulation is entirely voluntary. Any issuer can still sell a bond and label it "green" using existing market standards (like ICMA's Green Bond Principles) or self-defined criteria, as long as it does not use the specific European Green Bond (EuGB) label. Only bonds that carry the EuGB label must meet full EU Taxonomy alignment. This means an investor can still buy a "green" bond that funds gas or nuclear projects, but that bond cannot be called a European Green Bond.
title: European Green Bond Regulation (2023/2631/EU)

question: Does the EU Green Bond label allow bonds that fund nuclear or gas power?
answer: A European Green Bond cannot fund new nuclear or gas power plants, because those activities do not meet the Taxonomy's "do no significant harm" criteria for the label. However, an ordinary "green" bond sold to investors in the EU can still fund nuclear or gas under the Taxonomy's complementary delegated act — it just cannot use the EuGB label. This was the core political compromise that allowed the regulation to pass in late 2023, as it avoided a direct ban on nuclear and gas financing while creating a pure label that excludes them.
title: European Green Bond Regulation (2023/2631/EU)

question: Will existing green bonds need to be re-issued to become European Green Bonds?
answer: No. The regulation applies only to bonds issued on or after the application date of December 21, 2024. A bond issued before that date and marketed as "green" will not automatically become a EuGB. If an issuer wants to label an existing bond as a European Green Bond, it would need to cancel the old bond, issue a new one with the required Taxonomy-aligned prospectus and external verification, and meet all post-issuance reporting obligations — a costly process few issuers are expected to undertake. The label is only meaningful for new issuance.
title: European Green Bond Regulation (2023/2631/EU)

question: Why did European green bonds need a separate standard at all?
answer: Before 2024, the green bond market was a patchwork of voluntary standards, each with different criteria. A bond sold as "green" under ICMA's principles could fund activities that a bond under the Climate Bonds Standard would prohibit, making it impossible for investors to compare offerings reliably. The lack of a single enforceable definition created a risk of widespread greenwashing, which undermined investor confidence precisely when the EU needed massive private capital to finance its €1 trillion European Green Deal. The EuGB label provides a single, auditable, legally enforceable benchmark against which all other "green" claims can be measured — even if the label itself is voluntary.
title: European Green Bond Regulation (2023/2631/EU)

question: Can companies label their bonds as "green" without this standard?
answer: Yes — the EuGB label is voluntary. Companies can still issue bonds called "green" under national laws or market standards like the ICMA Green Bond Principles. But those bonds cannot be called "European Green Bonds" or use the EuGB logo. The regulation only applies if a company chooses to use the label.
title: European Green Bond Regulation (2023/2631/EU)

question: Why did Europe need a specific law for green bonds?
answer: Because the green bond market had grown to over €200 billion in the EU by 2020 with no consistent definition of what counted as "green." The European Court of Auditors found that some issuers were labelling bonds as green while financing fossil fuel projects. Banks and investors wanted a single, legally enforceable standard so they could compare bonds across countries and be confident the money went to actual environmental projects.
title: European Green Bond Regulation (2023/2631/EU)

question: Is nuclear power allowed under the EU Green Bond label?
answer: Yes, but only in limited circumstances. The regulation defines "taxonomy-aligned" by reference to the EU Taxonomy Regulation, which includes nuclear and natural gas activities under strict conditions. A EuGB bond could finance a nuclear power plant that meets the Taxonomy's emissions and safety criteria, but not conventional fossil fuel infrastructure. This has been highly controversial among environmental groups.
title: European Green Bond Regulation (2023/2631/EU)

question: What happens if an issuer misuses the EuGB label?
answer: ESMA can fine the issuer up to 10% of the bond's face value for intentionally misusing the label. For external reviewers that certify the bonds, ESMA can impose fines of up to €500,000 and withdraw their registration entirely. The regulation also grants investors the right to claim damages if they relied on a false EuGB certification when buying a bond.
title: European Green Bond Regulation (2023/2631/EU)

question: Can a victim of domestic violence keep a restraining order when moving to another EU country?
answer: Yes, under the European Protection Order Directive. The victim's home country issues a standardised certificate (Annex I) that the receiving EU state must enforce, typically by issuing a parallel national order. The protection lasts as long as the original order remains valid. Victims should inform their local police or victim support organisation before moving, as proactive notification is required — the receiving state cannot act unless it receives the certificate.
title: European Protection Order Directive (2011/99/EU)

question: Why did the EU need a special law for cross-border protection orders?
answer: Before 2011, no mechanism existed for mutual recognition of criminal protection orders across EU borders. An abuser could simply follow a victim to another member state, where the domestic restraining order had no legal effect. The "Ruth case" in 2007 — where a Spanish protection order was unenforceable in the Netherlands — exposed this gap. The Lisbon Treaty (2009) gave the EU the power to act, and the directive was designed to plug this hole without requiring member states to harmonise their criminal laws.
title: European Protection Order Directive (2011/99/EU)

question: Which European countries have used the directive the most?
answer: According to the Commission's 2021 evaluation, Spain, Germany, and the Netherlands have transmitted the highest numbers of orders (around 15–20 each by 2020). France and Italy have received the most orders. The overall number remains low — only 74 orders transmitted across all member states by 2020 — partly due to low awareness among police and victims. Eastern European states like Poland and Romania had initial compliance issues but have since adopted implementing legislation.
title: European Protection Order Directive (2011/99/EU)

question: Is the directive still relevant after Brexit?
answer: The directive continues to apply in the 27 EU member states. The UK, which had opted into the directive, left it with Brexit — meaning protection orders from the UK are no longer automatically recognised in the EU, and vice versa. The EU and UK have not negotiated a replacement mechanism, though bilateral agreements exist in some cases (e.g., Ireland and the UK have a reciprocal arrangement under the 1968 Brussels Convention). The directive remains operative within the EU, and the Commission has proposed strengthening it in its 2023 EU strategy on combating violence against women.
title: European Protection Order Directive (2011/99/EU)

question: Does a restraining order from one EU country work in another?
answer: Yes, but only if it was issued by a criminal court and has been formally transmitted to the destination country’s authorities. The victim (or the issuing court) must request the transmission, and the process can take weeks. Protection orders from civil courts (like many in Germany and Austria) are not covered — those require a separate procedure under the Brussels Regulation on civil matters.
title: European Protection Order Directive (2011/99/EU)

question: Why would someone need a cross-border protection order in the EU?
answer: Domestic violence victims often flee to another EU country to escape their abuser — for example, a Polish woman moving to Sweden. Under the directive, the Swedish police can enforce the Polish restraining order, banning the abuser from approaching her home or workplace in Sweden, without her starting a new case in Swedish courts.
title: European Protection Order Directive (2011/99/EU)

question: What countries do not fully follow this directive?
answer: As of the Commission’s 2017 evaluation, Bulgaria, Cyprus, Greece, Slovenia, and Malta had significant compliance gaps. The UK (before Brexit) and Ireland had opt-outs. In practice, even compliant countries face problems with police training — a 2019 study found that fewer than 30% of EU police officers knew the directive existed.
title: European Protection Order Directive (2011/99/EU)

question: Can a protection order be enforced against a perpetrator who moves to another EU country?
answer: Yes, that is the directive’s purpose. If a perpetrator knows a protection order exists, and they travel to the country where the victim now lives, the local authorities can arrest them for violating the order. However, enforcement depends on the perpetrator being identified and located — the directive does not create an EU-wide register of protection orders.
title: European Protection Order Directive (2011/99/EU)

question: Why did EU rules on large financial groups need updating after 2008?
answer: The 2008 crisis exposed that regulators were each looking at only part of a financial group — banking supervisors watched the bank, insurance supervisors watched the insurer — but nobody watched the whole group. When Fortis, which combined both activities under one parent company, ran into trouble in 2008, no single regulator had the power to demand capital at the group level. The same capital was sometimes counted twice, once for the bank and once for the insurer.
title: Financial Conglomerates Directive update

question: Does this directive set specific capital requirements for cross-sector financial groups?
answer: No — it requires member states to identify these groups and impose supplementary supervision, but the actual capital levels are set in sector-specific rules like the Capital Requirements Directive (for banks) and Solvency II (for insurers). The directive ensures regulators talk to each other and that risks from one part of the group aren't hidden from the other. The European Banking Authority and the European Insurance Authority later issued joint guidelines to harmonise how capital is calculated across sectors.
title: Financial Conglomerates Directive update

question: Which companies were most affected by these rules?
answer: Large bancassurance groups — firms that own both a bank and an insurance company — were the primary targets. ING Group (Netherlands), Fortis/BNP Paribas Fortis (Belgium), Allianz (Germany, with its banking arm), and Crédit Agricole (France, with its insurance arm) all had to adjust their internal capital modelling and reporting. The directive also caught non-European groups operating in the EU, such as Swiss Re and Zurich Insurance, where their operations crossed sector boundaries.
title: Financial Conglomerates Directive update

question: Were there any enforcement actions or penalties under this directive?
answer: The directive itself was supervisory framework — it didn't carry direct penalties. However, the European Banking Authority (EBA) conducted a peer review in 2018 that found several countries were still failing to identify all conglomerates. Specifically, the EBA's October 2018 report showed that Ireland, Luxembourg, and Malta had identified zero conglomerates despite having significant cross-sector financial activity, raising questions about enforcement. The Commission opened infringement procedures against three member states for late transposition in 2014.
title: Financial Conglomerates Directive update

question: Why did Europe rewrite its product safety rules for the internet age?
answer: The old 2001 directive assumed products were sold in physical shops by identifiable EU companies. But by 2017, two-thirds of Europeans shopped online, and dangerous goods — from exploding hoverboards to toxic toys — were flooding in from third-country sellers with no EU presence. The 2001 law couldn't hold marketplaces accountable for products they effectively sold. After a series of fires and recall failures, the EU decided marketplaces needed the same safety obligations as physical retailers.
title: General Product Safety Regulation (2023/988/EU) — online marketplace duties and product recall powers

question: How can an online marketplace be forced to remove a dangerous product?
answer: National market surveillance authorities can now issue a direct takedown order to a marketplace — no court order needed. The marketplace must remove or disable access to the listing within two business days. If the marketplace believes the order is wrong, it can appeal, but must remove the listing first. This "notice-and-takedown" system is modelled on the Digital Services Act, but for products instead of content.
title: General Product Safety Regulation (2023/988/EU) — online marketplace duties and product recall powers

question: Why can't consumers be reached directly when a product they bought is dangerous?
answer: That's exactly what the GPSR fixes. Previously, companies could fulfil their recall obligations by publishing a notice on a website or in a newspaper. The GPSR requires companies to contact customers directly — by email, text, or app notification — if they have the contact information. For products bought as guests without accounts, marketplaces must ask customers to register for recall alerts. In its impact assessment, the Commission found that fewer than 1 in 10 consumers received a direct recall notice for online purchases.
title: General Product Safety Regulation (2023/988/EU) — online marketplace duties and product recall powers

question: What happens if a marketplace disobey a takedown order?
answer: Member states set their own penalties, but the regulation requires fines to be "effective, proportionate, and dissuasive" — meaning they must hurt enough to change behaviour. The maximum penalty for a serious breach can be up to 4% of annual turnover in the member state concerned. For a platform like Amazon, which generated €50 billion in European revenue in 2022, that could mean a fine of up to €2 billion for a single violation.
title: General Product Safety Regulation (2023/988/EU) — online marketplace duties and product recall powers

question: Are online marketplaces now legally responsible for what people sell on them?
answer: Yes, for the first time. The GPSR treats Amazon, Temu, AliExpress, and similar platforms as "economic operators" — not just hosting intermediaries. They must appoint a single EU-based legal representative, remove dangerous goods when notified by safety authorities, and trace sellers. If a marketplace cannot identify the seller, it must remove all of that seller's listings. This overturns the previous safe-harbour approach from the 2000 E-Commerce Directive.
title: General Product Safety Regulation (2023/988/EU)

question: Who can order a product recall in the EU now?
answer: The European Commission can issue a direct recall order for products sold across multiple member states — a single binding decision that all 27 countries must enforce. Before the GPSR, a recall required separate action by each national authority, which often meant dangerous products remained on shelves in other EU countries for weeks or months. The Commission exercised this power for the first time in January 2025.
title: General Product Safety Regulation (2023/988/EU)

question: What products are affected by the new rules?
answer: The regulation applies to virtually all non-food consumer products sold in the EU — from electronics and toys to clothing and cosmetics. High-risk categories (like children's products, electronics, and PPE) require a digital "product passport" with traceability records. Food, medicines, and medical devices remain covered by their own specific regulations (like the General Food Law or the Medical Devices Regulation).
title: General Product Safety Regulation (2023/988/EU)

question: How do sellers outside the EU comply?
answer: Any non-EU manufacturer or seller must appoint a legal representative established in the EU — a person or company that can be held legally responsible for the product's safety. Goods sold via online marketplaces also require the marketplace to act as the responsible operator if the seller does not provide traceable contact details. This has forced major Chinese platforms like Temu and Shein to establish EU-based subsidiaries.
title: General Product Safety Regulation (2023/988/EU)

question: Why did Europe overhaul its rules for medical diagnostic tests?
answer: The old system let manufacturers self-certify most diagnostic tests — including those for cancer, HIV, and genetic disorders — without any independent review of their clinical accuracy. This was exposed as dangerously lax after investigations found that high-risk genetic tests were being sold in Europe without any proof they actually worked properly. The Poly Implant Prothèse breast implant scandal in 2012 broke the political dam, as it showed that the entire EU medical device oversight system — not just for implants — was built on trust in manufacturers rather than independent verification.
title: In Vitro Diagnostic Medical Devices Regulation (2017/746/EU)

question: What changed for a company that makes a COVID-19 test or a blood glucose monitor?
answer: Under the old rules, most tests were "self-declared" by the manufacturer — they could slap a CE mark on the product and start selling it across Europe without anyone checking their clinical data. Under the new rules, the company must now submit detailed evidence of clinical performance and scientific validity to an independent "notified body" for review. A COVID-19 antigen test, for example, would likely be Class C (high risk) and require an audit of its sensitivity and specificity data before it can be sold.
title: In Vitro Diagnostic Medical Devices Regulation (2017/746/EU)

question: Why are some diagnostic tests disappearing from European markets?
answer: Compliance with the new regulation is expensive — estimates from industry associations suggest certifying a single test can cost over half a million euros. Many smaller companies, particularly those offering tests for rare diseases that only sell a few hundred kits a year, cannot afford this. They've withdrawn their products from the EU market entirely, sometimes leaving patients and doctors without access to specialised diagnostic tools. The European Commission has acknowledged this is an unintended consequence, but insists the trade-off is necessary for patient safety.
title: In Vitro Diagnostic Medical Devices Regulation (2017/746/EU)

question: Are the rules applied the same way across all EU countries?
answer: Not entirely. While the regulation is directly binding, its implementation depends on the capacity of each country's "notified bodies" and local competent authorities. As of 2024, Germany and the Netherlands had designated notified bodies, while several smaller EU member states had none. This creates an uneven playing field: a manufacturer in Spain must send its device to a notified body in another country, adding time and cost. The Commission's EUDAMED database, designed to centralise registration and surveillance, was still not fully operational in 2024, further complicating cross-border oversight.
title: In Vitro Diagnostic Medical Devices Regulation (2017/746/EU)

question: Do bigger farms in the EU now face stricter pollution rules than small farms?
answer: Yes. For the first time, the recast sets binding Best Available Technique emission limits for intensive cattle farms with over 350 livestock units (roughly 250 cows), pig farms, and poultry farms. Smaller farms are exempt unless they operate near protected areas and cause significant pollution. Medium-sized farms (150-350 units) can opt in for a simpler permit process.
title: Industrial Emissions Directive recast (2024/1785/EU) — tougher pollution controls and permit digitisation

question: Why does EU law still allow some industrial sites to pollute for another decade after new rules take effect?
answer: The recast gives existing installations 4 years to comply with new BAT conclusions, and cattle farms a 17-year transition until 2040. This long timeline was a political compromise necessary to pass the law — farming states required it to avoid abrupt closures. New installations must also comply immediately, but existing ones get phased compliance windows.
title: Industrial Emissions Directive recast (2024/1785/EU) — tougher pollution controls and permit digitisation

question: Can citizens now challenge industrial permits more easily in the EU?
answer: Yes. The recast strengthens public participation rights by requiring permit authorities to consult the public early in the process — before the permit is drafted, not just after. Citizens and NGOs can now submit comments on the draft conditions, and authorities must respond in writing. Non-compliance with this early consultation requirement can invalidate the permit in court, as confirmed by the 2023 Court of Justice ruling on the Austrian cement case.
title: Industrial Emissions Directive recast (2024/1785/EU) — tougher pollution controls and permit digitisation

question: What happens if a country ignores these new pollution rules?
answer: The recast introduces a minimum penalty framework: for serious violations that cause harm to human health or the environment, Member States must impose fines that are "at least equivalent to the economic benefit" of non-compliance. The Commission can also initiate infringement proceedings and refer cases to the European Court of Justice, which can impose lump sum or penalty payments. In practice, enforcement will depend on national courts — the minimum penalty standard is new and untested, so early cases (expected in Germany, Poland, and France) will determine its teeth.
title: Industrial Emissions Directive recast (2024/1785/EU) — tougher pollution controls and permit digitisation

question: Does EU law now regulate factory farming for the first time?
answer: Yes, but only for the largest operations. The recast brings farms with at least 16,000 pigs, 3,000 pigs for breeding, 700 broiler chickens or 350 laying hens under EU pollution permit rules for the first time. Smaller farms and cattle operations remain exempt. The threshold is a compromise: the European Parliament called for 8,000 pigs and 300 broiler chickens, while the Council wanted no farm extension at all. The resulting rules apply to about 16% of EU pig farms and 12% of poultry farms — the ones responsible for the vast majority of ammonia emissions.
title: Industrial Emissions Directive recast (2024/1785/EU)

question: Why is the EU making companies submit "transition plans" for pollution?
answer: The recast requires each industrial installation to produce a plan showing how it will reach zero pollution by 2050, with intermediate targets for 2030 and 2040. This mirrors the corporate sustainability reporting directive (CSRD) requirements for climate transition plans. The idea is to force operators to think long-term rather than just meeting next year's permit limits. If an installation fails to submit a credible plan, the permitting authority can deny or revoke its permit. Environmental groups like ClientEarth and the European Environmental Bureau successfully lobbied for this provision, arguing that traditional BAT-based permits leave companies with no incentive to innovate beyond current best practice.
title: Industrial Emissions Directive recast (2024/1785/EU)

question: How will digital permits work in practice?
answer: By 2030, every EU member state must operate a digital system where industrial operators can apply for pollution permits, submit emissions data, and receive automated compliance checks against the most current BAT conclusions. This breaks with the paper-based system in which permits legally required physical signatures and took 3-5 years per installation to renew. The digital shift allows cross-border data sharing through the E-PRTR portal, meaning a company with installations in three countries can manage permits from a single dashboard. The fly in the ointment is interoperability: 14 member states currently use completely different databases for permit tracking, and the Commission has not yet funded the standardisation connectors required.
title: Industrial Emissions Directive recast (2024/1785/EU)

question: Can a bank charge me extra for sending money instantly?
answer: No. The regulation bans any fee for instant payments that exceeds the fee for a standard credit transfer. If your bank charges €0 for a standard transfer, instant transfers must also be free. If they charge €1 for standard transfers, instant transfers cannot cost more than €1. This ended the practice of many banks charging €5-10 for real-time transfers, a premium of up to 500%.
title: Instant Payments Regulation: Regulation (EU) 2024/886 — mandatory real-time euro transfers in the EU

question: Does every EU country have to allow instant transfers from my bank account?
answer: Yes, but with a timeline. All euro-area member states (20 countries) must comply by October 2025. Non-euro EU countries (Denmark, Sweden, Poland, Hungary, the Czech Republic, Bulgaria, Romania) have until July 2027 to offer outgoing euro instant payments, and they can choose not to join the euro instant scheme if their domestic currency is not covered. But if you hold a euro account in, say, Sweden, it must be reachable for instant payments from Germany by July 2026.
title: Instant Payments Regulation: Regulation (EU) 2024/886 — mandatory real-time euro transfers in the EU

question: What happens if my bank doesn't offer instant payments by the deadline?
answer: Your bank will be in violation of EU law and can face fines from its national regulator — in Germany, BaFin can impose penalties up to 10% of the bank's annual turnover. More practically, the bank will be forced to stop processing your standard euro transfers until it upgrades its systems, because the regulation prohibits offering only slow transfers after the deadline. This effectively compels compliance within the transition period.
title: Instant Payments Regulation: Regulation (EU) 2024/886 — mandatory real-time euro transfers in the EU

question: Is my money safer with instant payments than with a regular bank transfer?
answer: Not automatically. The regulation requires a new "confirmation of payee" check — your bank must verify that the IBAN and recipient name match before the transfer is sent, reducing the risk of misdirected payments. This is similar to the UK's Confirmation of Payee system, which has reduced fraud by about 30%. However, instant transfers remain irreversible: if you send money to a scammer, your bank cannot reverse it. Unlike credit card transactions (which have chargeback rights under the Payment Services Directive), instant transfers have no statutory fraud reimbursement. Consumer groups are pressing the Commission to address this gap.
title: Instant Payments Regulation: Regulation (EU) 2024/886 — mandatory real-time euro transfers in the EU

question: Why were breast implants with industrial silicone allowed on the EU market for nearly a decade?
answer: Under the old Medical Devices Directive (93/42/EEC), notified bodies — private companies hired and paid by device manufacturers — were responsible for certification. They audited quality management systems and documents, but tested neither raw materials nor final products. PIP’s notified body, TÜV Rheinland, conducted all its audits at PIP’s offices and never independently verified the silicone used. No EU system existed to cross-check or challenge such certifications until the MDR introduced mandatory unannounced factory inspections and material testing for high-risk devices.
title: Medical Devices Regulation (2017/745/EU)

question: Does the MDR make it harder for new medical devices to reach patients?
answer: Yes — by design. The MDR requires clinical investigations for high-risk devices rather than allowing “equivalence” claims to earlier devices. Certification times have roughly doubled, from 12-18 months to 24-36 months, and compliance costs have risen significantly. The European Commission has acknowledged that some device types — especially for rare diseases — are now less commercially viable. However, the MDR also includes a “clinical evaluation consultation procedure” for the highest-risk devices, which forces manufacturers to produce robust clinical data before market entry.
title: Medical Devices Regulation (2017/745/EU)

question: Can patients with faulty medical devices now be traced?
answer: The MDR introduces a Unique Device Identification (UDI) system that requires each device — and each implantable device implant — to carry a unique identifier. By 2027, all implantable devices must be identifiable through EUDAMED, the centralised database, allowing authorities to trace which patients received which specific device. This directly addresses the PIP scandal, where thousands of patients could not be identified because no traceability system existed.
title: Medical Devices Regulation (2017/745/EU)

question: Are notified bodies still paid by the manufacturers they certify?
answer: Notified bodies still receive fees from manufacturers, but the MDR imposes strict conflicts of interest rules. Notified bodies must now have separate audit teams, conduct unannounced factory inspections at least once every 12 months, and cannot have any financial or organisational link to the manufacturers they certify. The European Commission can also suspend or withdraw a notified body’s designation if it fails to meet requirements.
title: Medical Devices Regulation (2017/745/EU)

question: Why did it take a breast implant scandal to overhaul medical device safety in Europe?
answer: Europe's old device directives were built on voluntary standards and self-declaration by manufacturers, with oversight outsourced to private, for-profit companies (notified bodies). The system assumed that industry could regulate itself. PIP implants were certified by TÜV Rheinland — a German company that received fees from PIP — and those certifications were accepted by all EU countries without question. The fraud continued for nearly a decade because no national regulator had the authority or resources to inspect a certified manufacturer in another country. The scandal directly proved that the system's core assumption was false.
title: Medical Devices Regulation (2017/745/EU)

question: Does the MDR require more testing than the old rules?
answer: Yes, substantially more. Under the old directives, manufacturers could claim "equivalence" to an existing device to avoid conducting new clinical investigations — a loophole often exploited. The MDR requires manufacturers of implantable and Class III devices to conduct clinical investigations unless they can demonstrate that using existing clinical data is scientifically justified. For the first time, high-risk devices must be reviewed by independent expert panels before certification. The regulation also requires manufacturers to show not just safety but "clinical performance" — meaning a device must actually do what it claims.
title: Medical Devices Regulation (2017/745/EU)

question: Can a device still be certified if it doesn't meet MDR requirements?
answer: No — and that is the point. But the transition was messy. Because notified bodies were so slow to be designated, and because certification processes took 12–18 months, many perfectly safe legacy devices (including essential items like defibrillators, hip implants, and surgical instruments) lost their certificates temporarily. The Commission was forced to introduce grace periods and phase-out extensions in 2023 to avoid shortages. Critics like the European Consumer Organisation (BEUC) argued the Commission was reopening dangerous loopholes — the Commission insisted the delays were procedural, not about safety.
title: Medical Devices Regulation (2017/745/EU)

question: Are there still problems with EU medical device regulation today?
answer: Yes, multiple. As of 2024, EUDAMED — the central public database meant to provide full transparency — still has not been fully implemented. Only its first two modules (actor registration and device registration) are operational; the modules for clinical investigations and vigilance are delayed. The MDR also did not address the fundamental profit incentive of notified bodies — they are still paid by the manufacturers they certify, creating the same conflict of interest as before. The number of notified bodies has stabilised but remains low, causing bottlenecks. Some patient groups and MEPs have called for a fully public, EU-run certification authority, but the Commission proposed only further reforms in its 2023 MDR evaluation, not a fundamental redesign.
title: Medical Devices Regulation (2017/745/EU)

question: Why did the EU choose a single binding law for medical devices instead of letting each country adapt its own rules?
answer: A regulation is directly binding in all member states without national transposition, preventing the fragmented implementation that plagued the MDD. The MDD had been interpreted differently across countries — for example, the UK's MHRA permitted less rigorous clinical evidence than Germany's BfArM. The PIP scandal showed that a directive allowed notified bodies to operate under inconsistent national rules, with TÜV Rheinland's lax inspections in Germany going unpunished by regulators in France, where the devices were sold.
title: Medical Devices Regulation (MDR)

question: Why did new EU safety rules for medical devices lead to shortages of things like pacemakers and hip implants?
answer: The MDR demanded far more clinical evidence — for many devices, full clinical trials where the MDD had allowed literature reviews or small studies. Notified bodies, the private organisations that certify devices, were also required to meet stricter standards (more expert staff, unannounced inspections, liability insurance). Many existing notified bodies simply applied for designation under the MDR and failed to qualify; the Commission's 2022 report showed only 22 had been designated by 2024. This created a capacity crisis where manufacturers waited 12-18 months for certifications, forcing them to withdraw products that could not afford the wait or the cost.
title: Medical Devices Regulation (MDR)

question: Why did the faulty breast implant scandal not trigger stricter EU safety rules for medical devices sooner?
answer: The PIP scandal broke in 2010, but the legislative process took seven years because of intense industry lobbying and technical complexity. The European Parliament wanted a "strict liability" regime for notified bodies, while the Council (member states) and the Commission initially favoured a lighter touch. The metal-on-metal hip scandal of 2012-2013, which killed the "self-certification" model for hip implants, provided the political momentum to overcome industry objections. A key turning point was the 2013 loss-of-innocence report by the UK's Standard Procedure Committee, which found that DePuy's hip system had been certified with "inadequate clinical evidence" under the MDD.
title: Medical Devices Regulation (MDR)

question: How do Europe's medical device safety rules differ from the US FDA's approach?
answer: The FDA requires pre-market approval for high-risk devices (Class III) with independent agency review, while the MDR relies on notified bodies — private, for-profit organisations — designated by national governments. The FDA also mandates device tracking and recalls more aggressively. However, the MDR now requires significantly more clinical evidence for all devices than the MDD did, bringing it closer to the FDA's threshold. A key difference: the MDR applies to a broader range of "non-medical" devices like coloured contact lenses and liposuction cannulae, which are not regulated as medical devices in the US.
title: Medical Devices Regulation (MDR)

question: Does every EU country now have to restore its nature?
answer: Yes, but with significant flexibility. Each member state must submit a National Restoration Plan by 2026 showing how it will meet EU-wide targets for specific habitats — wetlands, forests, rivers, urban green space, and pollinators. But the regulation does not set uniform per-country targets for every habitat type; countries can negotiate which ecosystems to prioritise, as long as the EU collectively meets the 20% by 2030 aim. The Commission reviews plans and can request revisions, but cannot unilaterally impose a plan on a refusing member state.
title: Nature Restoration Regulation (2024/1991/EU)

question: Can farmers be forced to rewet their land under this law?
answer: Only indirectly, and not immediately. The regulation requires member states to restore drained peatlands (which are often agricultural land), but the most controversial provisions — requiring restoration on 50% of peatland area under agricultural use — were delayed in the final text. Countries must begin restoring 30% of organic soils by 2030, but the agricultural use provision applies only from 2035, and only on sites where restoration is "technically feasible without significantly impacting food production." Some countries (the Netherlands, Ireland, Germany) may still need to impose restrictions on farming drained peatlands, but the law leaves that decision to national governments, not Brussels.
title: Nature Restoration Regulation (2024/1991/EU)

question: Why did this law almost fail in the European Parliament?
answer: Because the centre-right European People's Party (EPP) — the largest group — opposed it from the start, arguing it would hurt farmers and landowners. The EPP tried to kill the bill entirely in a July 2023 vote, but failed by just 36 votes (336-300). The pro-restoration coalition consisted of Socialists & Democrats (S&D), the Greens/EFA, Renew Europe, and The Left — plus about 50 defectors from the EPP who broke party lines. Without those defectors — many from German and French delegations — the law would not have passed. The narrow vote forced the Commission to accept weaker targets in trilogue.
title: Nature Restoration Regulation (2024/1991/EU)

question: What happens if a country simply ignores its restoration obligations?
answer: The Commission can launch infringement proceedings at the Court of Justice of the EU, but only if the member state fails to *submit* a National Restoration Plan — not for missing the actual restoration targets. This was a deliberate compromise to get the law passed. If the Court finds a country in violation of its obligation to submit a plan, it can impose fines. But if a country submits a weak plan and then fails to meet it, the Commission's legal options are very limited — it must prove the country "manifestly failed" to implement its own plan, which is a much higher legal bar. Environmental lawyers have warned this enforcement gap could make the regulation toothless.
title: Nature Restoration Regulation (2024/1991/EU)

question: Does the EU now require countries to actively restore nature, not just protect it?
answer: Yes. The regulation shifts EU biodiversity policy from passive protection (prohibiting destruction of existing habitats) to active restoration (requiring member states to bring degraded areas back to healthy condition). This is a fundamental departure from the Birds and Habitats Directives, which only required member states to prevent further damage. Under the new law, a country cannot simply maintain the status quo—it must improve the health of its ecosystems measurably.
title: Nature Restoration Regulation (2024/1991/EU)

question: Can a country stop restoration if it threatens food production?
answer: The regulation includes a "brake" clause (Article 11) that allows a member state to suspend specific restoration targets—particularly agricultural ecosystem targets like rewetting drained peatlands—if the Commission declares that implementation would reduce agricultural land availability by more than 5% at the national level. However, this brake is temporary and must be accompanied by an alternative plan. Environmental NGOs like **WWF Europe** have warned the brake could become a permanent loophole; agricultural associations have demanded its activation immediately after the 2026 national plans are submitted.
title: Nature Restoration Regulation (2024/1991/EU)

question: How does the EU enforce nature restoration across 27 countries with very different ecologies?
answer: The regulation sets EU-wide targets but allows each member state to design its own restoration plan, tailored to its specific ecosystems (e.g., Finland focuses on boreal forests and peatlands; Greece on Mediterranean shrublands and coastal wetlands). The Commission reviews plans for "sufficiency" and can request revisions. If a member state fails to achieve its targets, the Commission can launch infringement proceedings at the European Court of Justice, with potential fines. The key challenge—already visible in the first year—is that legal targets for 2030 are still years away, making detection of non-compliance difficult until 2029–2030.
title: Nature Restoration Regulation (2024/1991/EU)

question: Why did farmers and their political allies oppose this law so fiercely?
answer: Agricultural associations, led by **Copa-Cogeca** and **Farm Europe**, argued that restoration—particularly rewetting drained peatlands for agriculture—would reduce the land area available for food production by up to 10% in some member states (e.g., the Netherlands, Ireland). They claimed this would increase food imports and harm EU food sovereignty. The **European People's Party** made this the core of their opposition in the Parliament, voting unanimously to reject the bill in June 2023. However, the compromise included the agricultural brake and a delay in peatland targets, which failed to fully satisfy farm groups but won enough support from member states like Germany and Spain where farmers were already receiving subsidies for "greening" measures under the Common Agricultural Policy.
title: Nature Restoration Regulation (2024/1991/EU)

question: Why can't online stores in Europe just put huge fake discounts on products anymore?
answer: EU law now requires that any price reduction show the lowest price the item sold for in the previous 30 days. This means a store cannot invent a fake "original price" to make a discount look bigger. The rule applies to all online sales within the EU and was passed because consumer agencies found around 40% of online discounts were deceptive.
title: Omnibus Directive (2019/2161/EU) — modern consumer rules for online marketplaces and fake discounts

question: What must online marketplaces like Amazon or eBay tell buyers about the seller?
answer: The Omnibus Directive forces online platforms to clearly disclose who the actual seller is — the platform itself or a third-party merchant. If it is a third party, the platform must state the seller's name, address, and legal status. This was introduced because consumers had no way to know whether they were buying from Amazon or a random reseller, making returns and complaints nearly impossible.
title: Omnibus Directive (2019/2161/EU) — modern consumer rules for online marketplaces and fake discounts

question: Does the EU have special rules for "free" apps and services that collect user data?
answer: Yes. The 2019 Omnibus Directive extended consumer rights to cover free digital services where users pay with their personal data instead of money. This means that free apps, social media platforms, and email providers must give users the same pre-contractual information (about features, termination rights) as paid services, following the Cambridge Analytica scandal which showed users had no contractual protections.
title: Omnibus Directive (2019/2161/EU) — modern consumer rules for online marketplaces and fake discounts

question: What changed about penalties for companies breaking EU consumer rules across multiple countries?
answer: The maximum fine for widespread EU-wide consumer violations was increased to 4% of the company's annual turnover in the affected member state(s). Before the Omnibus Directive, penalties varied wildly by country — from a few thousand euros in smaller states to €50,000 in Germany — making it cheaper to break rules than comply. The new ceiling was designed specifically to deter large digital platforms from ignoring consumer law.
title: Omnibus Directive (2019/2161/EU) — modern consumer rules for online marketplaces and fake discounts

question: Why did the Volkswagen scandal lead to new EU consumer protection rules instead of just using existing environmental or competition laws?
answer: The Volkswagen scandal was the catalyst because it demonstrated concretely that European consumer enforcement was structurally incapable of deterring large-scale, pan-European corporate violations. German consumer authorities admitted in internal memos that maximum fines of €100,000 — representing roughly 0.0002% of Volkswagen's €213 billion annual revenue — were "completely inadequate." The scandal was not about environmental damages but about the failure of consumer protection systems to match market reality.
title: Omnibus Directive (Better Enforcement and Modernisation)

question: Why do EU rules now cover "free" digital services like social media and cloud storage, even though consumers don't pay money for them?
answer: The Commission's 2018 impact assessment argued that consumers provide personal data as counter-performance when using "free" platforms like Facebook, Google, and Instagram, and should therefore be entitled to the same consumer rights as paying customers. The inclusion was pushed by MEP Evelyne Gebhardt and supported by the S&D group, over opposition from DigitalEurope and Google, which argued in a March 2018 submission that "users of free services are not consumers in the legal sense." The final text applied consumer rights to digital services where "the consumer pays with personal data."
title: Omnibus Directive (Better Enforcement and Modernisation)

question: Why didn't the EU create one single agency to enforce consumer rights across all member countries?
answer: The Commission initially considered creating a European Consumer Enforcement Authority modelled on the European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) or the European Competition Network, but this was rejected by the Council. A June 2018 Council legal opinion concluded that EU consumer enforcement lacked the legal basis under Article 114 TFEU for a centralised enforcement agency. Instead, the directive strengthened the Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) Regulation from 2017, which had created a network of national authorities. The compromise allowed for harmonised penalties without establishing new EU-level institutions.
title: Omnibus Directive (Better Enforcement and Modernisation)

question: Why did the EU decide to specifically crack down on companies selling lower-quality versions of the same product in different countries?
answer: The issue gained prominence through a 2016 study by the Slovakian consumer authority, which found that identical-brand products (including Coca-Cola, Nestlé coffee, and Pringles) had different compositions in Eastern versus Western Europe. Commissioner Jourová called a 2017 summit with food manufacturers in Brussels, where PepsiCo and Unilever publicly denied the practice. The European Consumer Organisation (BEUC) tested 128 products across 12 member states in 2018 and found 23% had materially different compositions under the same brand. The Western European industry associations — FoodDrinkEurope and AIM — lobbied against the prohibition, arguing that "market preferences legitimately vary," but the Commission concluded that the practice was misleading when packaging and branding were identical.
title: Omnibus Directive (Better Enforcement and Modernisation)

question: Can I get European weather data for free now?
answer: Yes, under the Open Data Directive, meteorological datasets are one of the six categories of high-value data that must be provided free of charge. This means national weather services like the UK Met Office, Germany's DWD, and France's Météo-France must make their core data — temperature, precipitation, wind speed, forecasts — available in machine-readable formats via API, without charging licensing fees. Some historical or high-resolution data may still be charged for under the "reasonable return on investment" exception, but basic datasets are now free.
title: Open Data Directive and Re-use of Public Sector Information (recast)

question: What data counts as "high-value"?
answer: The directive defines six categories: geospatial data (maps, addresses, cadastral parcels), earth observation and environment (land cover, water quality, air pollution), meteorological (temperature, precipitation, forecasts), statistics (GDP, population, employment by region), companies and company ownership (registration numbers, ownership structures, financial data), and mobility (public transport timetables, real-time traffic, road circulation). The 2021 implementing regulation further specifies which specific datasets within each category are mandatory. Member states can add more, but cannot remove any.
title: Open Data Directive and Re-use of Public Sector Information (recast)

question: Why was it so hard to get free public data before?
answer: The key reason was that many national public bodies — especially statistical offices, mapping agencies, and weather services — had business models built around selling data. For example, the German Federal Agency for Cartography and Geodesy earned millions of euros annually from licensing geospatial data. These bodies argued that revenue from data sales funded their operations and that free provision would require taxpayers to subsidise commercial re-users. The Commission’s impact assessment showed that these revenue streams were actually small relative to the potential economic benefits of open data, but the political fight was fierce because it threatened the institutional autonomy of these agencies.
title: Open Data Directive and Re-use of Public Sector Information (recast)

question: Does this directive apply to private companies?
answer: Only indirectly. The directive applies to "public sector bodies" — government agencies, ministries, state-owned enterprises, and public undertakings in transport, energy, and utilities. Private companies are not required to share their data under this directive. However, if a private company holds data that originates from a public sector body (e.g., a licensed version of a public map), that data may be subject to re-use rules if the public body originally collected it. The directive also encourages "data altruism" — voluntary sharing by private entities — but does not mandate it.
title: Open Data Directive and Re-use of Public Sector Information (recast)

question: Why does EU law now ban tiny shampoo bottles in hotels?
answer: The regulation bans single-use plastic packaging for toiletries in hotels and similar accommodations (under 50ml for liquids, 100g for solids). This is because such miniature packaging has a disproportionately high environmental footprint per unit of product — the packaging-to-content ratio is often 80% packaging to 20% product — and because reusable dispensers already exist as a technically and economically proven alternative. The ban covers an estimated 200 million such bottles placed on the EU market annually, according to the Commission's impact assessment.
title: Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (2025/40/EU)

question: Does this regulation force all EU businesses to accept reusable packaging?
answer: No. The PPWR mandates reuse targets only for specific sectors where reuse systems are already demonstrated to work: beverage containers (20% by 2030), transport packaging like pallets and crates (10%), and grouped packaging like shrink-wrap on multi-packs (10%). For most other packaging types, including food packaging and consumer goods, the regulation instead requires that packaging be "recyclable by design" — meaning it must be technically capable of being recycled, not that it must be reused. Businesses in the affected sectors are not forced to accept reusable packaging from others, but must offer customers the option of refillable containers if they sell beverages above the 2030 threshold.
title: Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (2025/40/EU)

question: What happens to a company that violates these packaging rules?
answer: The regulation empowers national authorities to impose fines of up to 4% of a company's annual EU turnover for systematic non-compliance — targeting repeated violations like using banned single-use formats or placing non-recyclable packaging on the market after 2030. For less serious first-time offences, member states must set maximum fines of at least €1,000 per violation, with the revenue going toward waste prevention programmes. The Commission also gains the power to temporarily ban non-compliant packaging types across the entire single market through emergency procedures — a power that, if used, would directly affect companies like Amazon, which ships over 6 billion packages annually within the EU.
title: Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (2025/40/EU)

question: Why did this take 30 years to update the original 1994 packaging law?
answer: The original 1994 directive was a product of its time — focused on recycling as a harmonisation measure for the single market, not waste prevention. Three factors blocked revision for three decades: first, the packaging industry's sustained lobbying against any mandatory reduction targets (Europen spent an estimated €8 million annually on Brussels representation); second, the diversity of member state systems (Germany's Green Dot system versus France's eco-modulation versus Eastern Europe's lack of infrastructure) made harmonisation politically fraught; and third, each Commission president from 1994 to 2022 prioritised other environmental files (climate, air quality, chemicals) over packaging waste. The breakthrough came only when the 2018 Circular Economy Action Plan, combined with data showing packaging waste rising despite all previous efforts, created an evidence base strong enough to overcome industry resistance — and when Virginijus Sinkevičius as Environment Commissioner made it his personal priority starting in 2022.
title: Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (2025/40/EU)

question: Why does Europe have rules on how big my product's packaging can be?
answer: The regulation aims to stop "overpackaging" — where products are sold in packages with excessive empty space, typically to make them look larger or for marketing purposes. EU studies from 2022 found that up to 30% of packaging volume in e-commerce consisted of void fill. The 40% empty space limit forces companies to design packaging that fits the product, reducing waste and transport emissions.
title: Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (2025/40/EU)

question: Will I have to return my pizza box or cola bottle to the shop?
answer: For some packaging, yes. The regulation requires reuse targets for transport packaging (pallets, crates) and beverage containers. In practice, this means you may see returnable systems for online deliveries by 2030, where boxes are collected for reuse. For beverage containers, deposit return schemes will become mandatory across all EU countries by 2029 — you pay a small deposit (typically €0.10-0.25) when buying a bottle or can, which you get back when you return it to a collection point.
title: Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (2025/40/EU)

question: Are miniature shampoo bottles in hotels really banned?
answer: Yes. From February 2026, hotels cannot offer single-use plastic packaging for toiletries under 50ml — effectively banning those tiny shampoo, conditioner, and soap bottles. Hotels must switch to refillable dispensers mounted in bathrooms. The regulation exempts medically necessary products and solid soaps. The hospitality industry estimated this would save 800 million such bottles from landfill annually across the EU, based on figures from the European Hotel and Restaurant Association.
title: Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (2025/40/EU)

question: Does the regulation apply to imported products sold in the EU?
answer: Yes. The regulation applies to all packaging placed on the EU market, regardless of origin. Importers are responsible for ensuring their products' packaging meets the recyclability and minimisation requirements. This creates a significant compliance burden for non-EU manufacturers, particularly from China and the US, who must redesign packaging for the EU market or face import bans from February 2026. The Commission estimated that 15% of non-food consumer goods imported from outside the EU would require packaging redesign.
title: Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (2025/40/EU)

question: Why did the EU switch from letting each country write its own packaging rules to having one set of rules for everyone?
answer: The 1994 directive allowed member states significant leeway, creating 27 different national systems that made cross-border trade costly. A 2021 study by the European Court of Auditors found that national transposition led to €1.9 billion in additional administrative costs annually for businesses operating in multiple member states. The regulation’s direct applicability removes these barriers and ensures uniform rules across the single market.
title: Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation

question: Why are tiny single-use containers like hotel mini-shampoo bottles being banned?
answer: The ban targets packaging formats that are disproportionately wasteful relative to their utility. According to the Commission’s 2022 impact assessment, mini-shampoo bottles account for only 0.5% of total hotel packaging but generate 10 times the waste per use of larger formats. The EEB argued that such bans also serve to shift consumer behaviour by eliminating the convenience of single-use packaging in hospitality.
title: Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation

question: How will companies meet the requirement to use a certain amount of recycled material in their packaging?
answer: The regulation allows companies to meet recycled content targets through a system of compliance credits traded among industry players, similar to the approach in the Single-Use Plastics Directive (2019/904). The Commission’s 2023 implementing acts specify that recycled content must come from post-consumer waste, not production scrap, and that chemical recycling counts only if it meets life-cycle analysis standards set by the European Chemicals Agency (ECHA).
title: Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation

question: Can you sue a company for harm caused by an AI or software bug in the EU?
answer: Yes, definitively. The recast Directive explicitly classifies software, AI models, and digital files as "products" under strict liability rules. A consumer who suffers harm from a defective navigation app, a flawed medical AI, or a smart home device hacked due to security flaws can now bring a claim without having to prove the manufacturer was negligent — only that the product was defective and caused the harm.
title: Product Liability Directive (2024/2853/EU) — software and AI now covered by strict liability rules

question: Does this mean every AI chatbot or app developer needs product liability insurance?
answer: Not every small app developer — but anyone placing an AI product on the EU market commercially almost certainly does. The directive exempts non-commercial open-source AI development, but once it's sold or used in a professional context, strict liability applies. The Commission's impact assessment noted that liability insurance premiums for AI products could increase 20-40%, and the directive specifically requires that claims be "viable" through sufficient insurance coverage for commercial operators.
title: Product Liability Directive (2024/2853/EU) — software and AI now covered by strict liability rules

question: Why did EU law change its definition of "product" after 40 years?
answer: Because the 1985 Directive was written for the physical world — typewriters, washing machines, and cars. The 2015 Volkswagen Dieselgate scandal was the moment EU regulators realised software that deliberately manipulated emissions tests was not legally a "product" that could be sued for. Then came the explosion of smart devices, AI diagnostics, and over-the-air software updates, each exposing the same gap. The ECJ's own rulings in 2019 showed courts were hopelessly divided on whether software counted. The pandemic, with its health-app failures, broke the political deadlock.
title: Product Liability Directive (2024/2853/EU) — software and AI now covered by strict liability rules

question: Who opposed this change and why?
answer: The software and tech industry, represented by **DigitalEurope** (Google, Apple, Microsoft, Siemens), fought it hard, warning that strict liability would make innovation "legally toxic" for small AI startups. German industry group **BDI** argued software was a service, not a product. The opposition was intense enough to delay the Commission's proposal for over a year, but the final vote in the European Parliament was 637-21, showing the political will was overwhelming. The industry's main concession was the exemption for non-commercial open-source development.
title: Product Liability Directive (2024/2853/EU) — software and AI now covered by strict liability rules

question: Can a landlord in the EU refuse to rent an apartment to someone because of their race or ethnicity?
answer: No. The Racial Equality Directive explicitly prohibits discrimination based on race or ethnic origin in access to housing, including rental properties. EU member states must ensure that landlords, real estate agents, and housing authorities cannot refuse to rent, impose worse conditions, or evict someone because of their ethnic background. However, enforcement varies — housing discrimination can be difficult to prove, and in some countries, equality bodies lack the resources to investigate complaints effectively.
title: Racial Equality Directive (2000/43/EC)

question: Does EU law protect against racial discrimination beyond the workplace?
answer: Yes, and this is what makes the 2000 directive groundbreaking. Unlike earlier EU anti-discrimination laws that only covered employment, the Racial Equality Directive covers social protection (including healthcare and social security), education, and access to goods and services (including housing, banking, and public transport). This broader scope was a deliberate choice by the European Commission and the member states after the Amsterdam Treaty, and it remains wider than comparable protections for other grounds (religion, disability, age, and sexual orientation) which are still limited to employment under the parallel Employment Equality Directive (2000/78/EC).
title: Racial Equality Directive (2000/43/EC)

question: Why did the EU need its own law on racial discrimination when member states already had national laws?
answer: Before 2000, EU law contained no explicit prohibition of racial discrimination — only a ban on nationality-based discrimination, which primarily protected EU citizens moving between member states. This meant that a Romanian citizen working in Germany was protected from being treated worse than a German citizen, but a German citizen of Turkish origin had no EU-level protection against racial discrimination by their employer. The Racial Equality Directive filled this gap and also set a common minimum standard across the EU, preventing a race to the bottom where countries with weaker protections could attract employers.
title: Racial Equality Directive (2000/43/EC)

question: Does the directive require member states to set up an official equality body?
answer: Yes. Article 13 of the directive obliges all member states to designate one or more bodies with a mandate to promote equal treatment, analyse discrimination, conduct independent surveys, and publish reports. These equality bodies — such as the UK's Equality and Human Rights Commission, France's Défenseur des Droits, or Germany's Antidiskriminierungsstelle des Bundes — must be independent of government influence and have the power to assist victims of discrimination in pursuing complaints. The Commission's 2014 report found that while all member states had established such bodies, their independence, resources, and enforcement powers varied considerably.
title: Racial Equality Directive (2000/43/EC)

question: Why do EU anti-discrimination laws protect against racism based on race and ethnicity, but not against discrimination based on nationality?
answer: The Treaty of Amsterdam deliberately limited the Directive's scope to race and ethnicity because nationality discrimination is already governed by separate EU rules on free movement of workers and EU citizenship. Extending the Directive to cover nationality would have created conflict with Member States' immigration policies. The Commission's 2000 proposal explicitly stated that "differences of treatment based on nationality" are excluded, preserving Member States' control over third-country nationals' access to employment and social benefits.
title: Racial Equality Directive

question: Why was the EU’s ban on racial discrimination passed in just four months after the far-right entered Austria’s government?
answer: The political pressure from the Austrian sanctions created an unprecedented window of opportunity. The Portuguese Presidency saw the Directive as a direct response to Haider, and Commissioner Diamantopoulou fast-tracked the proposal through the Commission's internal procedures.
